attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# Synology Monitor
2	
3	Telemetry collection, issue detection, and LLM-driven remediation for a two-unit Synology NAS fleet. A Go agent on each NAS pushes metrics and logs to Supabase; a Next.js dashboard groups them into issues and runs a three-stage AI pipeline that diagnoses problems and proposes fixes behind an operator approval gate. An MCP server also exposes 100+ NAS diagnostic tools to AI chat clients.
4	
5	Live at **[mon.designflow.app](https://mon.designflow.app)**.
6	
7	## Docs
8	
9	| | |
10	|---|---|
11	| [AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md) | **Start here** — canonical operating guide for AI sessions and engineers |
12	| [docs/architecture.md](docs/architecture.md) | System design, data flow, component constraints |
13	| [docs/development.md](docs/development.md) | Build, run, test, debug |
14	| [docs/configuration.md](docs/configuration.md) | All environment variables |
15	| [docs/deployment.md](docs/deployment.md) | CI/CD and release workflow |
16	| [PLAN.md](PLAN.md) | Historical issue-agent rebuild plan; current behavior is documented in `docs/architecture.md` |
17	
18	## Prerequisites
19	
20	- **Go 1.23+** with CGO enabled
21	- **Node.js 22+** and **pnpm 9**
22	- **Docker**
23	
24	## Getting started
25	
26	```sh
27	pnpm install
28	pnpm build
29	```
30	
31	| Task | Command |
32	|---|---|
33	| Run web app locally | `cd apps/web && pnpm dev` (needs `.env.local` — see `docs/development.md`) |
34	| Build the Go agent | `cd apps/agent && CGO_ENABLED=1 go build ./...` |
35	| Run type checks | `pnpm type-check` |
36	| Run Go tests | `cd apps/agent && go test ./...` |
37	
38	## Where to look for what
39	
40	| You want to... | Start here |
41	|---|---|
42	| Add or modify a NAS diagnostic tool | `packages/shared/src/nas-tools.ts`, `apps/nas-mcp/tools-config.json` |
43	| Add a new agent telemetry collector | `apps/agent/internal/collector/` + wire in `cmd/agent/main.go` |
44	| Change how issues are detected or diagnosed | `apps/web/src/lib/server/issue-detector.ts`, `ai/pipeline-v2.ts` |
45	| Allow a new NAS shell command | `apps/nas-api/internal/validator/validator.go` + `validator_test.go` |
46	
47	## Deployment
48	
49	Push to `main`. GitHub Actions builds and pushes images to GHCR. The web app and NAS MCP server redeploy automatically via Coolify webhook; the agent and NAS API are picked up by Watchtower on each NAS within 5 minutes.
50	
51	That covers ordinary image deploys. Compose changes such as new mounts, capabilities, or env keys require an explicit `docker compose up -d` on the NAS after the file is updated there, because Watchtower does not apply compose-file changes. See [docs/deployment.md](docs/deployment.md) for rollback, pinning, and the exceptional manual-compose path.
52

1	# Synology Monitor — Agent & Developer Operating Guide
2	
3	Canonical guide for AI sessions and new engineers. Read this first. Deeper
4	references: [docs/architecture.md](docs/architecture.md),
5	[docs/development.md](docs/development.md),
6	[docs/configuration.md](docs/configuration.md),
7	[docs/deployment.md](docs/deployment.md). The 3-stage issue-agent rebuild is
8	complete (2026-05-30); current behavior and durable rationale live in
9	[docs/architecture.md](docs/architecture.md). [PLAN.md](PLAN.md) is historical.
10	
11	There is no universal ignore-file standard across AI coding tools.
12	
13	`.claudeignore` works for Claude Code.
14	
15	When using any other AI tool, paste this file as your first message and follow the instructions in the "What to ignore" section.
16	
17	## 1. Project summary
18	
19	AI-assisted monitoring + remediation for two production Synology NAS boxes
20	(`edgesynology1`, `edgesynology2`). A Go agent on each NAS collects telemetry into
21	Supabase; a Next.js dashboard (live at **mon.designflow.app**) fingerprints alerts
22	and logs into "issues" and runs a 3-stage LLM issue-agent that diagnoses problems
23	and proposes fixes behind an operator approval gate. A separate MCP server exposes
24	NAS diagnostic/remediation tools to AI chat clients (claude.ai, Claude Desktop).
25	Product focus: Synology Drive / ShareSync reliability, file-operation visibility,
26	sync/replication failures, storage and I/O attribution, and silent task/backup
27	failures. The owner is a non-developer; favor changes that keep `main` the single
28	source of truth and are easy to audit.
29	
30	## 2. Quick orientation — five components
31	
32	| Component | Language | Where it runs | Purpose |
33	|---|---|---|---|
34	| `apps/agent` | Go | each NAS (Docker) | Collects telemetry, buffers in SQLite WAL, flushes to Supabase |
35	| `apps/nas-api` | Go | each NAS (Docker, :7734) | Three-tier approved-shell-command executor for the issue agent + MCP |
36	| `apps/nas-mcp` | Node/TS | VPS (Coolify) | MCP server — exposes a 119-definition NAS tool registry to AI chat clients over Streamable HTTP/SSE |
37	| `apps/web` | Next.js | VPS (Coolify) | Dashboard, issue detector, 3-stage issue-agent loop, operator UI |
38	| `apps/relay` | Node (.mjs) | VPS | Narrow named-action HTTP proxy for an external (Lovable) frontend |
39	
40	`packages/shared` holds shared TypeScript types and the NAS tool definitions
41	(built with Turbo). `ALL_TOOL_DEFS` currently contains 119 registry definitions;
42	`restart_nas_api` is an extra always-on MCP tool implemented in
43	`apps/nas-mcp/src/index.ts`. **One branch: `main`.** Push to `main` → GitHub Actions builds
44	per-app images → web/nas-mcp auto-redeploy via Coolify webhook; agent/nas-api are
45	picked up by Watchtower on each NAS within ~5 min. **Supabase** (project
46	`qnjimovrsaacneqkggsn`) is the shared data layer between agent (writes) and web
47	(reads). NAS API does not touch Supabase.
48	
49	## 3. Repository structure
50	
51	```
52	apps/
53	  agent/        Go — collectors + DSM client + SQLite WAL sender   (we own)
54	  nas-api/      Go — validator (allowlist/hard-blocks) + executor  (we own)
55	  nas-mcp/      Node/TS — MCP server + 119-definition tool registry           (we own; dist/ generated)
56	  web/          Next.js — dashboard + issue agent                  (we own src/; .next/ generated)
57	  relay/        Node .mjs — named-action proxy                     (we own)
58	packages/shared/ shared TS types + NAS tool definitions            (we own src/; dist/ generated)
59	supabase/migrations/  DB schema (00001..00041) — applied history   (we own; do not rewrite applied files)
60	supabase/functions/send-push/  Deno edge function                  (we own)
61	deploy/synology/      NAS compose + per-NAS env examples           (we own)
62	.github/workflows/    4 image-build workflows                      (we own)
63	docs/                 architecture / development / configuration / deployment / mcp-incident
64	scripts/              backfill-synobackup.mjs, check-dashboard-data.mjs
65	PLAN.md               historical design doc for the completed 3-stage issue-agent rebuild
66	```
67	
68	Generated / not-source: `apps/web/.next/`, `apps/*/dist/`, `apps/nas-mcp/dist/`,
69	`packages/shared/dist/`, `.turbo/`, `node_modules/`. Vendored/framework: the
70	Next.js runtime surface under `apps/web/.next/`. Build artifacts: image layers (in
71	GHCR, not the repo).
72	
73	## 4. Prime directive — custom-code boundary
74	
75	Our custom code lives here:
76	
77	- `apps/agent/`, `apps/nas-api/`, `apps/nas-mcp/src/`, `apps/web/src/`, `apps/relay/`
78	- `packages/shared/src/`
79	- `supabase/migrations/`, `supabase/functions/`
80	- `deploy/synology/`, `.github/workflows/`, `docs/`, top-level `*.md`, `scripts/`
81	
82	Everything else (`node_modules/`, `.next/`, `dist/`, `.turbo/`, lockfiles, the
83	Next.js framework surface) requires explicit justification before touching. Do not
84	scatter project logic into generated or framework files.
85	
86	## 5. Core modification inventory
87	
88	No files outside the project-owned areas (above) have been patched — there is no
89	forked vendor/framework code in this repo. All code is first-party; third-party
90	code is consumed only as dependencies (`node_modules`, Go modules) and base Docker
91	images. If you ever patch a vendored file, record it here.
92	
93	| File | Change made | Why | Risk during upgrades |
94	|---|---|---|---|
95	| — | — | — | — |
96	
97	## 6. Task-to-file navigation
98	
99	| Task | Files to touch | Files NOT to touch |
100	|---|---|---|
101	| Add a NAS MCP tool | `packages/shared/src/nas-tools.ts` (def + `TOOL_GROUPS`), `apps/nas-mcp/tools-config.json` (enable) | `apps/nas-mcp/src/index.ts` (registry-driven; no registration needed) |
102	| Add an agent collector | `apps/agent/internal/collector/<name>.go`, wire in `apps/agent/cmd/agent/main.go` with the `wg.Add(1)` pattern | existing collectors |
103	| Send a new agent field to Supabase | `apps/agent/internal/sender/types.go` + a `Queue*` method, **and** a matching column via a new `supabase/migrations/*.sql` | applied migration files |
104	| Allow a new NAS command tier | `apps/nas-api/internal/validator/validator.go` (+ `validator_test.go`; Go/RE2 regex only, no lookaround/backrefs) | `executor.go` |
105	| Change an AI pipeline stage | `apps/web/src/lib/server/ai/stage{1,2,3}-*.ts`, `pipeline-v2.ts` | `issue-detector.ts` fingerprinting |
106	| Change AI model per stage | Settings UI (live dropdowns) → `ai_settings` table; fallback chain in `apps/web/src/lib/server/ai-settings.ts` | hardcoded defaults |
107	| Add a selectable model / fix its capabilities | nothing — dropdowns are live from connected providers (`provider-models.ts` → `/api/ai-models`). To **tune** a model's effort/tool-use precisely, add a row to `MODEL_CATALOG` in `packages/shared/src/ai-capabilities.ts` (overrides the heuristic) | the live-fetch list logic unless adding a provider |
108	| Add a new log source to the agent | `apps/agent/internal/logwatcher/watcher.go` (`defaultLogFiles`) | No source whitelist to update (migration 00035 dropped it) |
109	| Add an env var | `apps/web/.env.example` and `apps/web/src/app/api/settings/route.ts` if it's an AI setting, `docs/configuration.md` | production env (lives in Coolify or NAS `.env`) |
110	| Add a DB migration | `supabase/migrations/000NN_description.sql` — next number after current max (00041) | applied migrations |
111	| Add a dashboard page | `apps/web/src/app/(dashboard)/<page>/page.tsx`, hook in `src/hooks/` | — |
112	| Add a nightly custom command | Insert into `custom_metric_schedules` DB table with `collection_command`, `interval_minutes`, `nas_id` | — |
113	
114	## 7. Data model and external identifiers
115	
116	Do not casually rename or regenerate these.
117	
118	| Entity / System | Identifier | Where defined | Notes |
119	|---|---|---|---|
120	| Supabase project | `qnjimovrsaacneqkggsn` | Supabase | Postgres; 53 tables total |
121	| NAS 1 (`edgesynology1`) | id `4f1d7e2a-7d5d-4d5f-8b55-0f8efb0d1001`, Tailscale `100.107.131.35` | `deploy/synology/nas-1.env.example` | `nas_units.id` must match agent `NAS_ID` |
122	| NAS 2 (`edgesynology2`) | id `9dbd4646-5f4e-4fa0-8f44-1d0dbe6f1002`, Tailscale `100.107.131.36` | `deploy/synology/nas-2.env.example` | |
123	| NAS API port | `7734` | NAS `.env` (`NAS_API_PORT`) | HTTP over Tailscale |
124	| Coolify nas-mcp app | `efl17f5iocnz94840pexre9d` | **hardcoded** in `.github/workflows/nas-mcp-image.yml` | redeploy webhook target |
125	| Coolify web app | `${COOLIFY_WEBHOOK_UUID}` | GitHub secret (not in repo) | redeploy webhook target |
126	| Coolify API host | `http://178.156.180.212:8000` | both deploy workflows | VPS Coolify control plane |
127	| GHCR images | `ghcr.io/u2giants/synology-monitor-{agent,nas-api,nas-mcp,web}` | workflows | tags: `latest`, `sha-<sha>`, `main` |
128	| Public endpoints | `mon.designflow.app` (web), `nas-mcp.designflow.app/mcp` | Coolify/Traefik | |
129	
130	## 8. Container and service inventory
131	
132	| Container / service | Purpose | Managed by | App ID | Image / source |
133	|---|---|---|---|---|
134	| `synology-monitor-web` | Dashboard + issue agent | Coolify | `${COOLIFY_WEBHOOK_UUID}` (secret) | `ghcr.io/u2giants/synology-monitor-web:latest` |
135	| `synology-monitor-nas-mcp` | MCP server for AI chat clients | Coolify | `efl17f5iocnz94840pexre9d` | `ghcr.io/u2giants/synology-monitor-nas-mcp:latest` |
136	| `synology-monitor-relay` | External-client named-action proxy | Coolify (manual — see below) | — | `ghcr.io/u2giants/synology-monitor-relay` |
137	| `synology-monitor-agent` | Telemetry collector (per NAS) | Watchtower on NAS | — | `ghcr.io/u2giants/synology-monitor-agent:latest` |
138	| `synology-monitor-nas-api` | Approved-command executor (per NAS, :7734) | Watchtower on NAS | — | `ghcr.io/u2giants/synology-monitor-nas-api:latest` |
139	| `synology-monitor-watchtower` | Auto-updates agent + nas-api from GHCR (300s poll) | NAS compose | — | `containrrr/watchtower` |
140	| Supabase `qnjimovrsaacneqkggsn` | Telemetry + issue tables | Supabase | — | managed Postgres |
141	
142	**Relay has no CI workflow** — there is no `.github/workflows/relay-*.yml`. The
143	relay image is not produced by the standard pipeline; it is built/deployed manually
144	on the VPS. Treat its deploy path as exceptional, not routine.
145	
146	## 9. What to ignore
147	
148	Not relevant to active development; do not read or index (already in
149	`.claudeignore` / `.cursorignore` / `.copilotignore`): `node_modules/`, `apps/*/node_modules/`,
150	`.next/`, `dist/`, `apps/*/dist/`, `.turbo/`, `.cache/`, `coverage/`,
151	`*.tsbuildinfo`, `pnpm-lock.yaml`, `package-lock.json`, `**/*.bak`, `evals/` (unless working on
152	agent evaluation), and the vestigial scratch file `ersahazan2Desktopsynology-monitor`.
153	
154	## 10. Intentional quirks — do not "fix" these
155	
156	### NAS MCP is fully stateless (per-request McpServer)
157	Looks like: a bug — every HTTP request builds a new `McpServer`, registers tools,
158	handles the request, discards. No session map.
159	Actually: deliberate (`sessionIdGenerator: undefined`, `enableJsonResponse: true`).
160	Why: Coolify restarts wipe in-memory sessions; statelessness eliminates "stale
161	session 404" after redeploys, and a stateless transport for `GET /mcp` without a
162	session ID stopped the claude.ai proxy's 4-minute hang (see incidents).
163	Do not change because: stateful mode brings back session-resume bugs and forces
164	dynamic tool registration that Claude clients ignore.
165	
166	### NAS MCP exposes 5 tools but has a 119-definition registry
167	Looks like: most tools are broken/unregistered.
168	Actually: deliberate lazy-load. `tools/list` returns only `tool_search`,
169	`invoke_tool`, `run_command`, `check_disk_space`, `restart_nas_api`. Clients
170	discover via `tool_search`, execute via `invoke_tool({name,target,args})`.
171	Why: pre-loading 119 schemas put ~50k tokens into every session and degraded it
172	after ~10–15 calls; lazy-load keeps the always-on surface ~3k tokens.
173	Do not change because: it brings back session degradation. New always-on tools go
174	in `EAGER_TOOLS` in `index.ts`, accepting the context cost.
175	
176	NOTE — the "~10–15 calls" here is **context-token bloat only**, and it is already
177	fixed by lazy-load. It is NOT a per-session call quota, and it is NOT why the
178	command validator ever refuses a command. If a tool call gets *blocked* (vs. the
179	session getting vaguer), that is the stateless hard-block list below, not
180	degradation. Do not conflate the two — see "Recursive grep … is hard-blocked".
181	
182	`tools-config.json` currently has 80 read entries and 40 write entries. That total
183	(120) is not the same as `ALL_TOOL_DEFS` (119) because `restart_nas_api` is a
184	special always-on write tool implemented in `apps/nas-mcp/src/index.ts`, not in
185	the shared registry.
186	
187	### `Connection: close` on every nas-api request (from nas-mcp/web)
188	Looks like: throws away HTTP keep-alive.
189	Actually: required — timed-out requests don't return their socket to undici's pool,
190	so after ~10–15 calls the pool exhausts and calls hang. NAS API is local over
191	Tailscale (sub-ms RTT), so re-handshake cost is negligible. (This "~10–15" is the
192	undici socket pool — a third, unrelated meaning of that number, also already fixed.
193	It is not session degradation and not the validator.)
194	
195	### Node `keepAliveTimeout: 120s` / `headersTimeout: 125s` on nas-mcp
196	Set above Traefik's 90s idle timeout so Traefik never reuses a socket Node already
197	closed (fixed "Tool result could not be submitted").
198	
199	### Sender isolates one bad row instead of failing the whole batch
200	Looks like: extra complexity in `apps/agent/internal/sender/sender.go` (`postRows`).
201	Actually: required. PostgREST inserts a batch as one statement; one bad row rejects
202	all rows, and after 5 retries the WAL drops them. On a 4xx the sender re-sends each
203	row individually so good rows land and only the bad row is dropped.
204	Why: this exact failure silently froze log/alert ingestion for ~19h/23d (see
205	incidents). Do not revert to all-or-nothing batches.
206	
207	### No source whitelist on `nas_logs` / `alerts`
208	Looks like: missing validation.
209	Actually: the `*_source_check` CHECK constraints were dropped (migration 00035).
210	They had to be hand-expanded every time a collector added a source and caused the
211	ingestion outage. The agent governs what it writes; the sender isolates bad rows.
212	Do not re-add a source whitelist.
213	
214	### `check_backup_status` enumerates ~7 candidate log paths
215	Deliberate. On one NAS the canonical `synobackup.log` was stale (2024) while the
216	live log was in a per-task target dir. The tool lists every candidate with
217	mtime+size, tails the freshest, shows a staleness banner. A single-path bet returns
218	false-positive stale data.
219	
220	### NAS API package restarts use the DSM WebAPI, not `synoservice`
221	`synoservice` was removed in DSM 7; restarts go through `SYNO.Core.Package`
222	stop+start, requiring `DSM_USERNAME`/`DSM_PASSWORD` in the NAS `.env`.
223	
224	### Recursive grep on `@synologydrive` / `@SynologyDriveShareSync` is hard-blocked
225	A `grep -R` against Synology's internal stores ran 4d11h on production before
226	discovery. Blocked at the validator regardless of tier (see incidents).
227	
228	This block is **stateless and pattern-based** (`hardBlocked` in
229	`apps/nas-api/internal/validator/validator.go`). It fires on the exact same
230	command identically on call #1 and call #500 — there is no call counter and no
231	session state. If your `grep` got refused, you did NOT "use up a session" and the
232	session is NOT "degrading"; you issued a recursive grep against a Drive data store
233	and a permanent safety guard refused it. Do not retry it, do not "hand off to a
234	fresh session" expecting it to work, and do not record it as a degradation symptom.
235	
236	What to do instead when you need a "deeper log grep":
237	- Grep the *specific* log file, non-recursively: the Drive logs are known paths
238	  (`@synologydrive/log/*.log`, `syncfolder.log`) — see `logwatcher/watcher.go`.
239	- Use the purpose-built tools: `search_file_access_audit` (grep-based over raw DSM
240	  audit logs), `check_backup_status` (enumerates + tails backup logs).
241	- For anything historical, query the Postgres tables — that is what ingestion is
242	  for. A recursive grep over the raw stores never returns useful results anyway.
243	The block only matches `grep -r/-R` *together with* a Drive path; a bounded grep of
244	one named log file is tier-1 and auto-executes.
245	
246	### Executor kills the process group, not just bash
247	`Setpgid: true` + `syscall.Kill(-pid, SIGKILL)` + `WaitDelay: 2s`.
248	`exec.CommandContext` only kills the direct bash child, so `grep ... | head`
249	orphans `grep` on timeout. Combined with the hard-block, prevents the runaway.
250	
251	### Collector goroutines must use the WaitGroup pattern
252	`wg.Add(1)` + `defer wg.Done()`. Without it, graceful shutdown returns before the
253	collector finishes, dropping in-flight WAL writes (the ShareSync collector had this
254	bug, commit `268b9c9`).
255	
256	### `package_status` is the only merge-duplicates upsert
257	It's current-state (one row per NAS+package). All other telemetry tables are
258	append-only inserts.
259	
260	### DB tables are unprefixed; two functions still carry `smon_`
261	Migration 00031 renamed all `smon_*` tables. Migration 00034 renamed 4 standalone
262	functions. Two helper functions (`smon_create_alert`, `smon_get_openai_key`) are
263	intentionally still `smon_`-prefixed because other functions call them by name.
264	Historical migrations (00002–00030) still contain `smon_` — do not rewrite them.
265	
266	### HMAC approval tokens are never persisted
267	Stage 2 persists the action intent (command, tier, target, summary) but **never
268	the HMAC token**. The token is minted fresh at execution time in
269	`pipeline-v2.ts::executeApprovedAction`. Persisting tokens would cause 403s after
270	the 15-min expiry — the operator's approval window is often hours.
271	
272	### NAS API requires `apparmor=unconfined` + `SYS_ADMIN`
273	Looks like: insecure container config.
274	Actually: required by DSM. Synology's container runtime rejects the default
275	AppArmor profile during init; `SYS_ADMIN` is required for `btrfs subvolume list`,
276	scrub, and snapshot operations. DSM constraint, not a choice.
277	
278	### NAS API has `SYS_PTRACE` but `gdb` and `lldb` are hard-blocked
279	Looks like: contradictory — why grant ptrace and then block it?
280	Actually: `strace` and `/proc/PID/stack` (already readable with `SYS_ADMIN`) are the
281	safe ptrace operations. `gdb`/`lldb` are blocked because they can call arbitrary
282	functions in a traced process via `call system(...)` — a code-injection vector.
283	`strace_process` uses `-c` count mode only (syscall summary, no argument printing)
284	so no sensitive data is printed.
285	Do not change because: removing the `gdb`/`lldb` hard-blocks would allow an
286	AI-generated command to inject arbitrary code into live DSM processes.
287	
288	### `/dev/sd*` and `/dev/md*` are individually named mounts, not the full `/dev` tree
289	Looks like: there should just be `- /dev:/dev:ro`.
290	Actually: individual named mounts (`/dev/sda:/dev/sda:ro`, ..., `/dev/md3:/dev/md3:ro`).
291	Why: Docker bind-mounts the source device at container start. If a source device
292	doesn't exist (empty drive bay), the compose `up` fails for the whole service.
293	Individual mounts let you comment out non-existent bays.
294	Do not change because: mounting the full `/dev` tree read-only would still expose
295	`/dev/mem`, `/dev/kmem`, and other sensitive kernel interfaces.
296	
297	### Watchtower updates images but NOT compose configuration
298	Looks like: after pushing `docker-compose.agent.yml` changes to `main`, the NAS
299	containers will pick them up like code changes.
300	Actually: Watchtower pulls the new image and restarts with the **existing compose
301	state** — it reads the current in-memory config, not the file on disk. New
302	capabilities (`cap_add`), volume mounts, env keys, etc. do not take effect until
303	`docker compose up -d` is run manually on the NAS with the updated compose file.
304	Do not assume because: this is how Docker Compose restart semantics work — container
305	recreation from a new image uses the last-applied compose spec, not the repo copy.
306	
307	### AI-stage model dropdowns are live; `MODEL_CATALOG` is an override, not the menu
308	Looks like: `MODEL_CATALOG` in `packages/shared/src/ai-capabilities.ts` is the list
309	of selectable models, so a model missing from it (e.g. a new DeepSeek release)
310	can't be picked.
311	Actually: since 2026-06-02 the dropdowns are populated **live** from every connected
312	provider's list-models endpoint (`apps/web/src/lib/server/ai/provider-models.ts`,
313	served by `/api/ai-models`). `MODEL_CATALOG` is now a precise-metadata override +
314	offline/no-keys fallback. Catalog-miss ids get a *derived* descriptor (provider by
315	id prefix, cache style by provider, effort/tool-use by conservative id heuristics);
316	`callModel` resolves `catalog → derived → live-map` and only fails when no connected
317	provider offers the id.
318	Why: the curated list silently excluded any model not hand-added; the operator asked
319	for every connected provider's models to be selectable.
320	Do not change because: deleting `MODEL_CATALOG` or re-gating the dropdown to it
321	breaks precise effort/cache/tool-use metadata for the curated models and the
322	offline fallback. A selected model whose capabilities are derived is flagged with an
323	amber "inferred model" warning in the UI; to tune it exactly, add a catalog row.
324	
325	### `issue_evidence` and `issue_evidence_items` are different tables
326	Looks like: `issue_evidence_items` is just the renamed `issue_evidence`.
327	Actually: entirely different purposes.
328	- `issue_evidence` (created 00022): curated human-readable notes (title/detail)
329	  written by the copilot, resolution API, and `seedIssueFromOrigin`.
330	- `issue_evidence_items` (created 00038): the lossless telemetry store for the
331	  3-stage pipeline. Written by Stage 1 and Stage 2 tool calls.
332	Do not query the wrong one for a given context.
333	
334	### Drive client logs are at `/host/shares/@synologydrive/log/`
335	Looks like: the logwatcher's `WATCH_PATHS` default (`/host/volume1`) should cover
336	Drive logs.
337	Actually: the agent compose mounts `@synologydrive` at `/host/shares/@synologydrive`
338	(not under `/host/volume1`). `inferDriveLogFiles` prepends `/host/shares` first so
339	the glob resolves. The old watch-path attempt is kept as a harmless fallback.
340	
341	### `drive_team_folders_partitioned` has no child partitions and receives no writes
342	Looks like: dead schema artifact.
343	Actually: forward infrastructure for pg_partman partition management when
344	`drive_team_folders` grows large enough. No child partitions created yet.
345	Do not drop it.
346	
347	### `analyzeRecentLogs` (log-analyzer.ts) has no callers
348	Looks like: dead function.
349	Actually: orphaned when `/api/analysis` was rewritten to use `runIssueDetection`.
350	The `analysis_runs` and `analyzed_problems` tables remain for a potential future
351	AI clustering layer. The three former readers were migrated to `issues` (2026-05-31).
352	
353	### `getSecondOpinionModel` and `getClusterModel` are exported but have no callers
354	Looks like: dead exports.
355	Actually: planned features. `getSecondOpinionModel` is for a second AI model
356	cross-checking Stage 2 diagnoses; it is explicitly deferred.
357	`getClusterModel` is the intended abstraction for `log-analyzer.ts`. Keep both.
358	
359	### `/host/*` paths in agent container
360	Looks like: wrong paths.
361	Actually: the agent runs in Docker. The NAS host `/proc`, `/sys`, `/var/log` are
362	mounted read-only at `/host/proc`, `/host/sys`, `/host/log`. Shared folders are
363	at `/host/shares/<name>`. The `/host/` prefix keeps host and container namespaces
364	distinct. See `deploy/synology/docker-compose.agent.yml`.
365	
366	### NAS API mount layout is not the same as the agent mount layout
367	Looks like: a read-only diagnostic should find DSM package files at
368	`/host/var/packages` or snapshots under `/volume1`.
369	
370	Actually: the NAS API compose mounts host `/var/packages` at `/host/packages`, not
371	`/host/var/packages`. Full Btrfs data volumes are mounted at `/btrfs/volumeN` for
372	subvolume/snapshot commands; individual shared folders are mounted separately
373	under `/volume1/<share>` and may not expose system snapshot directories.
374	
375	Why: Synology Container Manager rejects some top-level volume binds during
376	compose/UI recreates. The compose file uses narrower named mounts for shares,
377	package state, host libraries, and Btrfs volumes.
378	
379	Do not change because: tools that only check `/volume1` or `/host/var/packages`
380	will miss Snapshot Replication state and scheduler/package artifacts on one NAS.
381	Read-only tools should check `/host/packages` and `/btrfs/volumeN`, and may use
382	DSM WebAPI read methods as a fallback when SQLite/config paths are not mounted.
383

410	## 12. Deployment
411	
412	Push to `main` → `.github/workflows/{agent,nas-api,nas-mcp,web}-image.yml` build
413	and push to GHCR (each has a `paths:` filter; tags `latest`, `sha-<sha>`, `main`):
414	
415	- **web** and **nas-mcp**: workflow's final step calls the Coolify redeploy webhook
416	  (`GET http://178.156.180.212:8000/api/v1/deploy?uuid=...` with `COOLIFY_TOKEN`).
417	  nas-mcp UUID is hardcoded (`efl17f5iocnz94840pexre9d`); web's is `$COOLIFY_WEBHOOK_UUID`.
418	- **agent** and **nas-api**: no webhook. Watchtower polls GHCR every 300s and
419	  recreates. `NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_*` is baked at image build — changing it in
420	  Coolify after build has no effect; requires a new push to `main`.
421	- **relay**: no workflow; built/deployed manually on the VPS (exceptional).
422	
423	Runtime env lives in **Coolify** (VPS) and each NAS `.env` (agent/nas-api).
424	Rollback: redeploy a previous tag in Coolify, or pin `AGENT_IMAGE_TAG` to a SHA
425	on the NAS, or `git revert` + push. **SSH is not a routine deploy path** — public
426	SSH on the VPS is disabled by design; manual container rebuilds create drift.

428	## 13. Critical incidents
429	
430	### 2026-05-29 — Log/alert ingestion silently frozen + pg_partman broken
431	What happened: `nas_logs` stopped ingesting ~19h, `alerts` ~23 days.
432	Root cause: source-check whitelists rejected newer log sources; one bad row failed
433	the whole PostgREST batch → dropped after 5 retries. partman config pointed at
434	pre-rename `smon_*` parent names.
435	Recovery: dropped whitelists (migration 00035); sender now isolates bad rows;
436	corrected partman, reclaimed 3.34 GB.
437	Rule: no source whitelists; sender must isolate bad rows; empty tables are bugs.
438	
439	### 2026-05-29 — Live secrets found committed in example/recovery files
440	What happened: real NAS API secrets, relay tokens, Supabase service-role key,
441	NAS SSH password committed in example and recovery files.
442	Recovery: values redacted; `.env.runtime` gitignored.
443	Rule: never commit real secrets. **Leaked values remain in git history and MUST
444	still be rotated by the owner.**
445	
446	### 2026-05 — 4-day runaway `grep -R` on production NAS
447	Fix: validator hard-block list + process-group kill in `executor.go`.
448	
449	### 2026-05 — Claude MCP sessions hanging / failing
450	Fix: stateless transport; `Connection: close`; `keepAliveTimeout 120s`.
451	Full writeup: [docs/mcp-incident-2026-05.md](docs/mcp-incident-2026-05.md).
452	
453	### 2026-06 — NAS API crash-loop from invalid Go regexp
454	Cause: validator used PCRE-style negative lookahead `(?!...)` inside
455	`regexp.MustCompile`; Go RE2 does not support lookaround, so `nas-api` panicked
456	at startup after Watchtower pulled the image. Symptom: MCP calls returned
457	`ECONNREFUSED` to both NAS `:7734` endpoints. Fix: use RE2-safe positive regexes
458	or Go helper code, add validator tests for both match and exception cases, then
459	verify `/health` on both NASes after `nas-api-image.yml` publishes.
460	
461	### 2026-06 — Read-only MCP probes missed DSM 7/NAS API paths
462	What happened: `check_scheduled_tasks`, `list_snapshot_candidates`, and
463	`inspect_snapshot_replication` were safe to run but reported missing data because
464	they only checked legacy or agent-style paths.
465	Impact: AI sessions could not confirm DSM scheduled tasks or Snapshot Replication
466	schedule/retention rules even though the package/runtime existed.
467	Root cause: NAS API mount layout differs from the telemetry agent: package state is
468	under `/host/packages`, and the full Btrfs volume is under `/btrfs/volumeN`.
469	Recovery: widened read-only path discovery, opened SQLite with `-readonly`, added
470	DSM WebAPI read fallbacks for task and Snapshot/Replication API discovery, and
471	kept writes/start/cancel operations separate.
472	Rule added to prevent recurrence: read-only NAS tools must be narrow and
473	allowlisted, but they must cover the actual compose mounts before assuming DSM data
474	is absent.
475	
476	### 2026-06 — Snapshot Replication probe exceeded NAS API command limit
477	What happened: `inspect_snapshot_replication` was read-only, but it bundled too
478	much DSM WebAPI/config/SQLite discovery into one generated shell probe. The NAS
479	API rejects commands over 4096 bytes, so the MCP tool failed before reaching
480	either NAS.
481	Recovery: keep `inspect_snapshot_replication` as a compact first-pass probe and
482	push deeper follow-up into separate read-only tools such as
483	`summarize_snapshots_by_share`, `check_scheduled_tasks`, and `fetch_package_db`.
484	Rule added to prevent recurrence: every generated NAS MCP command must stay under
485	the NAS API `maxCommandLength`; split broad diagnostics into smaller named tools
486	instead of raising the limit or packing everything into one shell command.
487	
488	### 2026-05 — `check_backup_status` returning stale 2024 data
489	Fix: multi-path freshest-by-mtime discovery + staleness banner.
490

1	# PLAN — Issue-Agent AI Rebuild (3-stage, cached, live-tool)
2	
3	Status: **BUILT, deployed, and live as the only issue-agent pipeline** (2026-05-30).
4	Authored 2026-05-29. All build-order steps (§10) shipped: config layer +
5	capability matrix, provider-native clients + caching core + CI guards, lossless
6	evidence store + Stage 1 + fetch_evidence, resumable turn state machine, Stage 2
7	reasoning core, Stage 3 explainer/memory, admin UI (3 stages + cache-hit + NAS
8	offline badge), §6 tool-catalog sharing into packages/shared, the cutover (v2 is
9	the default in code), and the cleanup (legacy 7-stage pipeline + OpenRouter
10	inference path removed). The sections below are the original design, kept for
11	reference. Provider keys (Anthropic/OpenAI/Gemini/DeepSeek/Qwen) are live in
12	Coolify. Outstanding (separate from this rebuild): rotate the leaked secrets
13	still in git history.
14	
15	**How to use this doc:** This is the self-contained handoff for a *fresh* coding
16	session (start clean — prompt caching makes re-reads cheap but does not stop a
17	cluttered context from degrading reasoning). Read `AGENTS.md` first for repo
18	rules, then this. It restates everything so the coder needs no prior conversation.
19	Where this doc and your assumptions conflict, trust this doc.
20	
21	---
22	
23	## 0. TL;DR
24	
25	Replace the current 7-call, OpenRouter-routed, no-caching, lossy issue-agent
26	pipeline with **3 config-driven stages**:
27	
28	1. **Lossless Structurer** (cheap) — de-noise/dedup raw telemetry into a complete,
29	   structured **evidence store persisted in the DB**. "Lossless" means no distinct
30	   event is discarded — *not* that everything is force-fed into one prompt.
31	2. **Reasoning Core** (strong, cached, live tools, resumable) — one agentic loop
32	   that holds a stable cached prefix + a **bounded** evidence slice, pulls more
33	   evidence/live NAS data **on demand** via tools, and resumes from DB state across
34	   approval gates. The "SSH-genius" brain, with guardrails.
35	3. **Explainer / Memory** (cheap) — operator reply + durable memory.
36	
37	Inference uses **provider-native SDKs directly** (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google Gemini,
38	DeepSeek, Qwen) — **never an aggregator** on the cached inference path — with
39	caching done correctly per provider (§9). Models + reasoning-effort are chosen by
40	the operator at runtime via an admin page (3 stages × 2 controls), gated by a
41	**provider capability matrix** (§8.3). The operator's choice always overrides, but
42	each stage keeps a hardcoded final-fallback default so a cold/empty `ai_settings`
43	still boots (§8.1).
44	
45	Five decisions this plan locks down (previously gaps): the **context budget**
46	(§5), the **tool integration boundary** (§6), the **approval/resume state machine**
47	(§7), the **config key-map + capability matrix** (§8), and **cache correctness
48	under resume** (§9). Stage 2's three invariants tie them together: it must be
49	**bounded**, **resumable from the DB**, and **cache-correctness-independent**.

53	## 1. Why we're doing this — full diagnosis
54	
55	The Issue Investigator feels far dumber than Claude with a live SSH session. When
56	you SSH, Claude has a flashlight and the keys to the room: any command, raw output,
57	iterative digging with a frontier model. The Investigator is the opposite, for six
58	verified reasons (all in `apps/web/src/lib/server/`):
59	
60	### 1a. It reads pre-stored telemetry, not live data — over fixed windows
61	`gatherTelemetryContext` (`issue-agent.ts`) reads Supabase and makes **zero live
62	NAS calls during reasoning**. Windows/limits as built:
63	
64	| source | window | limit | note |
65	|---|---|---|---|
66	| alerts | none | 12 | latest active |
67	| nas_logs (warning+) | 6h | 60 | excludes system/storage/scheduled_task/share_quota/share_health |
68	| nas_logs (audit, high-signal) | 48h | 80 | only those excluded sources |
69	| process_snapshots / disk_io_stats | 6h | 20 | |
70	| scheduled_tasks / storage_snapshots / dsm_errors | 48h | 20 / 20 / 30 | |
71	| backup_tasks | 6h | 30 | |
72	| snapshot_replicas / sync_task_snapshots | 6h | 20 / 15 | |
73	| container_io / metrics | 30m | 15 / 40 | |
74	
75	Freshness measured 2026-05-29 (before the ingestion fix): metrics ~30s fresh, but
76	logs were 19h stale and alerts 23 days stale (ingestion was broken — now fixed,
77	§4). The wins: keep ingestion healthy (done) **and** let Stage 2 pull
78	up-to-the-second data live via tools (this rebuild).
79	
80	### 1b. The "compression" is lossy (the core defect)
81	`compressLogsToFacts` (`issue-stage-models.ts`): slims each log to `{t,sev,msg}`
82	and **truncates msg to 200 chars**, caps input to ~140 rows (40/source), prompts
83	for **one baseline "pattern" fact per source** + one per anomaly (summarizing away
84	all non-anomalous detail), and the **raw `logs`/`audit_logs` are stripped before
85	hypothesis/planner** (`telemetryWithoutLogs` in `issue-agent.ts`). So the reasoning
86	models work from a lossy summary of stale data. Stage 1 fixes this — see §3/§5.
87	
88	### 1c. Free-form tools instead of a curated catalog
89	The Investigator does **not** use a fixed menu; the planner generates *arbitrary*
90	tier-1/2/3 shell commands gated only by the nas-api validator. It reinvents
91	commands rather than using the curated, battle-tested catalog
92	(`apps/nas-mcp/src/tool-definitions.ts`, 108 tools; the web app's own `tools.ts`
93	has ~42 for the legacy copilot). There is a `tool_gaps` output but **no
94	"what am I missing" step**, and no way to fetch live data mid-cycle except by
95	proposing a diagnostic action that costs a whole cycle. Fixed in §3/§6.
96	
97	### 1d. Narrow vision — pre-scoped to one fingerprint
98	`issue-detector.ts` fingerprints alerts+logs into issues (families:
99	sharesync-metadata-corruption, sharesync-api-invalid, drive-not-ready,
100	sync-failure, sync-conflict, thumbnail-extract-failure, backup-failure,
101	rename-activity; sustained I/O pressure ≥20% avg iowait / critical ≥40%;
102	`buildCorrelatedIncidentGroups` correlates drive/hyperbackup churn + snapshot
103	cleanup + I/O). The agent then sees only that slice. Fixed in §3 (whole-system
104	snapshot + sibling-issue visibility).
105	
106	### 1e. Re-chewing — no "nothing changed" detection
107	`runIssueAgent` (`MAX_AGENT_CYCLES = 8`) re-runs hypothesis→planner→explanation
108	each cycle with no check that the evidence set is unchanged; `hasAlreadyTried`
109	compares command *text* only. Fixed in §3 (re-chew guard).
110	
111	### 1f. No caching, and the prompts are cache-hostile
112	`callStageModel` uses the OpenAI SDK with `baseURL: openrouter.ai`, sends a single
113	user message `` `${backendFindings}\n\n${prompt}` `` with no `cache_control`, and
114	puts the semi-dynamic `backendFindings` **first** — so there is no stable prefix to
115	cache. Realizations (operator was right): stages **can** share a cache via a
116	byte-identical prefix; ~80–90% of each stage's context is identical; only the
117	trailing instruction differs. Fixed in §9.

162	
163	## 3. Target architecture — 3 stages
164	
165	Each stage reads **(model, reasoning/effort)** from config (§8). Caching (§9)
166	makes the strong middle stage affordable. Stage 2's invariants — **bounded,
167	resumable, cache-independent** — are defined in §5/§7/§9.
168	
169	### §3.1 Stage 1 — Lossless Structurer (deterministic, no model call)
170	
171	#### What this stage is doing
172	
173	Stage 1 is a deterministic data transformation pipeline — it makes **no model calls**. Its job is to convert raw Supabase telemetry into a structured, deduplicated, prioritised evidence store that Stage 2 can reason over without drowning in noise or losing signal. The core insight driving the design: the old `compressLogsToFacts` threw away information before Stage 2 ever saw it, silently hiding anomalies behind summaries. Stage 1 is the antidote — nothing distinct is discarded, but repetition is collapsed and the volume is managed before hitting a prompt. Think of it as a lossless event recorder followed by a smart index, not a summariser.
174	
175	The stage does five things in order:
176	
177	1. **Ingest.** Query Supabase across all telemetry source tables for the issue's time window. Gather every row and tag each with its evidence source label (see table below).
178	
179	2. **Deduplicate.** For each `(source, body)` pair, collapse byte-identical rows into one row with `dedup_count` tracking occurrence count and `first_ts`/`last_ts` spanning the range. Two rows are identical only if their entire bodies are the same — paraphrase-similar rows are never merged. The goal is to eliminate "same thing 4,000 times" noise without ever hiding a structurally different event.
180	
181	3. **Classify: anomalous.** Mark each row `anomalous=true` if severity is `error` or higher, OR if severity is `warning` and the body contains at least one state-change keyword: `failed`, `error`, `timeout`, `degraded`, `offline`, `crash`, `panic`, `rejected`, `aborted`, `stopped`, `restart`, and a few dozen more. The keyword list is conservative — false negatives (missed anomaly) are worse than false positives (extra rows in the priority tier).
182	
183	4. **Classify: in-scope.** Mark each row `in_scope=true` if the row's `nas_id` matches `issue.affected_nas`, OR severity is `error+`, OR the issue has no explicit `affected_nas`. The purpose: cross-NAS noise that is merely informational stays in the evidence store but ranks lower in the budget allocation.
184	
185	5. **Budget and persist.** Allocate `EVIDENCE_TOKEN_BUDGET = 12,000 tokens` (≈48,000 chars) across two tiers. Tier 1 (70% of budget): in-scope anomalous rows, full bodies, sorted by descending severity then recency. Tier 2 (30%): everything else as dedup-with-count one-liners. The evidence index (source × time-bucket × count summary) is always included and does not count against the budget — it is Stage 2's map of what else exists and how to fetch it. Delete existing `issue_evidence_items` rows for the issue before inserting — Stage 1 is idempotent and safe to re-run.
186	
187	**Telemetry sources ingested** (`TELEMETRY_SOURCES` constant):
188	
189	| Source key | Evidence source label | Why it matters |
190	|---|---|---|
191	| `alerts` | `alert` | Active alert severities; the primary issue-detection signal |
192	| `logs` / `audit_logs` | per-DB `source` field | Raw DSM log lines; the richest free-text signal |
193	| `top_processes` | `process_snapshot` | CPU/mem/IO hotspots; used to attribute resource contention |
194	| `disk_io` | `disk_io` | Per-device IOPS, throughput, latency, utilisation, queue depth |
195	| `scheduled_tasks_with_issues` | `scheduled_task` | Failed/overdue DSM tasks; often the root cause of backup/sync failures |
196	| `backup_tasks` | `backup_task` | Hyper Backup task status and timing |
197	| `snapshot_replicas` | `snapshot_replica` | Replication job status and error codes |
198	| `container_io_top` | `container_io` | Per-container I/O attribution; separates agent I/O from Drive I/O |
199	| `sharesync_tasks` | `sync_task` | ShareSync pair status, error codes, pending item counts |
200	| `io_pressure_metrics` | `io_metric` | Aggregate iowait and pressure over time |
201	| `storage_snapshots` | `storage_snapshot` | Space snapshot timeline; detects runaway growth |
202	| `dsm_errors` | `dsm_error` | Structured DSM error events from the system journal |
203	
204	**Evidence body construction (losslessness).** For log-type rows (`logs`, `audit_logs`, `dsm_errors`) the per-DB `source` field is the evidence source label and the `message` field is the body. For all other structured rows the body is the full JSON serialisation via `stableJson`, which strips only the top-level `metadata` key (fields already promoted to evidence columns) to avoid duplication without hiding data.
205	
206	**Overflow rule.** If the priority tier alone exceeds its 70% budget, include the full evidence index plus as many full bodies as fit, highest-priority first. Never silently drop bodies — the index ensures Stage 2 can always retrieve omitted items via `fetch_evidence`.
207	
208	#### Why there is no model call here
209	
210	Using a model to compress logs before the reasoner is the original defect (§1b). Stage 1 is deterministic because lossy compression is the enemy of diagnosis: a summarising model decides what matters before the reasoning model has formed a hypothesis, so it inevitably discards the evidence that falsifies the wrong hypothesis. The structurer's job is to preserve and organise, not to interpret. There is no interpretation task here that benefits from a model.
211	
212	---
213	
214	### §3.2 Stage 2 — Reasoning Core (strong model, agentic loop, resumable)
215	
216	#### What this stage is doing
217	
218	Stage 2 is the diagnostic mind of the pipeline. Its goal is to determine what is wrong with one or both Synology NAS units, why it is wrong, and either propose a safe remediation or produce a definitive "we can't fix this without operator input / more information" verdict. It does this by forming and iteratively testing hypotheses using a combination of stored telemetry and live NAS data, in the same way an expert engineer would reason through an incident with an SSH session and the logs in front of them.
219	
220	This is not a summarisation task, not a question-answering task, and not a classification task. It is **iterative multi-hypothesis investigation under uncertainty**, using active data gathering to rule hypotheses in or out. The model must:
221	
222	- Read a structured evidence slice and extract the meaningful signal — differentiating genuine anomalies from background noise and from DSM quirks that look like errors but are normal (e.g. the scheduled-task DSM error 103 on edgesynology1 that is a known blind spot, or the `container_status` CPU/mem always showing 0).
223	- Commit to a ranked hypothesis rather than hedging across all possibilities. Vague "it could be X or Y" turns are useless — the model should state its working hypothesis with a confidence level and identify the single most diagnostic piece of evidence it would need to confirm or refute it.
224	- Choose tools precisely. The 100+ curated predefined tools plus `run_command` and `fetch_evidence` give the model direct read access to nearly everything on the NAS. A poor model will spam tools randomly or re-run tools it already called. A good one will identify the exact file path, counter, or service log that would distinguish between two plausible root causes, call that one tool, read the result, and update its hypothesis.
225	- Know when it has enough. The model must recognise when the evidence is conclusive and stop gathering, rather than continuing to call tools to fill the turn cap. Premature stops and unnecessary loops are both failure modes.
226	- Respect tier boundaries. Write-capable commands are hard-blocked by the NAS validator. When a fix is warranted, the model proposes it as a structured remediation for operator approval — it does not attempt to execute the fix itself.
227	- Degrade gracefully when the NAS is offline. If `withNasReachability` returns `nas_unreachable`, the model must pivot to diagnosing from the stored evidence only via `fetch_evidence` and communicate to the operator what it can and cannot determine without live access.
228	- Know when to ask the operator. Some issues require context only a human has (is this backup expected to take 6 hours? is that ShareSync pair intentionally paused?). The model must recognise these cases and ask a specific, answerable question rather than producing a vague escalation.
229	
230	One job invocation = one turn. Turns are bounded by `TURN_CAP = 8`. The process dies at every approval/user gate and may resume in a different worker after arbitrary time; Stage 2 must be fully resumable from DB state only (§7).
231	
232	#### The investigation flow across turns
233	
234	A well-behaved investigation across up to 8 turns typically follows this shape:
235	
236	- **Turn 1 — Orient.** Read the evidence slice. Identify the highest-severity events, cross-reference with the whole-system snapshot (other open issues, NAS reachability, active alert counts). Form an initial ranked hypothesis list (usually 1–3 candidates). Pick the single most discriminating test and call exactly that tool or `fetch_evidence` aggregate query. Produce `decision=continue` with the current hypothesis and a brief rationale.
237	- **Turns 2–4 — Test and narrow.** Each turn: evaluate the new tool result against the hypothesis. If the result is consistent with hypothesis A and inconsistent with hypothesis B, update the hypothesis confidence and move to the next discriminating test. If the result is ambiguous, choose a more targeted follow-up. Tools called in earlier turns are already in `issue_evidence_items` and will appear in the evidence slice on the next turn — do not re-call a tool that already returned a result.
238	- **Turn N (diagnosis confident) — Produce verdict.** Once the evidence is conclusive: either `propose_remediation` (with a specific action, tier, rationale, and expected outcome) or `conclude_stuck` (with a clear statement of what is known, what is unknown, and what operator action is needed). Do not continue gathering evidence after the hypothesis is settled.
239	- **Turn N (needs operator input) — Ask precisely.** If the investigation hits a decision point requiring human context, produce `ask_user` with a specific, single-sentence question. Do not ask multiple questions at once; each approval/ask cycle costs operator attention.
240	
241	The re-chew guard enforces forward progress: if the evidence slice hash and the planned action are identical to the prior turn (nothing new was learned, nothing new was proposed), the repeat counter increments. After ≥ 2 consecutive identical turns, the outcome is overridden to `ask_user` automatically, because the model is stuck without saying so.
242	
243	#### Prompt structure (strictly stable→dynamic for cache correctness, §9)
244	
245	Every turn rebuilds the full prompt from DB state. The ordering is non-negotiable for cache hit rates:
246	
247	1. `[stable]` System prompt — role definition, operating rules, tier policy, tool inventory summary with usage guidance for `fetch_evidence`, `run_command`, and predefined tools
248	2. `[stable]` Output schema — the exact JSON decision format the model must produce
249	3. `[stable]` NAS taxonomy — the known issue families (ShareSync metadata corruption, sync-failure, Drive-not-ready, backup-failure, I/O pressure, etc.), DSM blind spots (error 103, container_status zero values, etc.), known NAS hardware profiles
250	4. `[semi-stable]` Whole-system snapshot — NAS reachability result from 3s `nas-api /health` probe, active alert counts by severity over 6h, list of open/running sibling issues with titles
251	5. `[semi-stable]` Issue summary — title, severity, status, affected NAS, current hypothesis field, any operator constraints from prior messages
252	6. `[dynamic]` Evidence slice — the prioritised budget-managed output of Stage 1, rebuilt on every turn as new `issue_evidence_items` rows (tool results) are appended
253	7. `[dynamic]` Prior transcript — loaded from `issue_messages` (cap: 60 messages); `role=agent` → `"assistant"`, `role=system` → prefixed `"[system] …"` user turn
254	8. `[dynamic]` Per-turn instruction — one of: initial investigation prompt, re-chew warning (evidence unchanged, change your approach), or respond-to-user (operator replied)
255	
256	**Tool catalog (all tier-1, auto-execute):**
257	
258	| Tool | What the model uses it for |
259	|---|---|
260	| `fetch_evidence` | Reading the full lossless evidence store beyond what fits in the slice. The model should aggregate first (`group_by: source` or `group_by: time_bucket`) to see the shape of the data before paging into raw rows. Runs against Supabase, not the NAS — always available even when the NAS is offline. |
261	| `run_command` | Free-form read-only shell command on a specific NAS target: `cat /proc/mdstat` (RAID state), `cat /sys/block/md5/inflight` (live I/O gauge), `tail -n 200 /var/log/kern.log`, `cat /proc/net/dev`, etc. For situations where no predefined tool covers the exact file or command needed. Write commands are hard-blocked by the NAS validator regardless of what the model requests. |
262	| Predefined tools (100+) | Curated read-only diagnostic commands covering SMART health, BTRFS scrub/device status, ShareSync task detail, Hyper Backup job logs, Docker container state, top-process snapshots, network interface stats, package health, filesystem health, kernel I/O errors, and more. The model should prefer these over `run_command` when they cover the query, because they have tuned timeouts, known output formats, and tested behaviour. |
263	
264	Every tool result is persisted to `issue_evidence_items` immediately after execution. The evidence slice on the next turn will include these new rows, which means the re-chew fingerprint changes and the model is working from a genuinely larger evidence base.
265	
266	#### What model capabilities this stage requires — and which matter most
267	
268	Stage 2 is the most capability-demanding model call in the pipeline. The right model for this stage has:
269	
270	**Strong multi-step reasoning (critical).** The model must hold a hypothesis, identify what would falsify it, select the right tool call, evaluate the result against the hypothesis, and update its belief — across up to 8 turns, with a growing context. This requires genuine systematic reasoning, not pattern-matching to a likely answer. Models that produce fluent-sounding responses without real logical chains will confidently propose wrong remediations. Extended thinking / chain-of-thought modes directly improve performance here: the harder the issue (multi-causal, rare DSM behaviour, cascading RAID + sync + backup failure), the more the model benefits from spending tokens on internal reasoning before producing its output. For Anthropic models, `extended_thinking` with a meaningful `budget_tokens` value is recommended for this stage. For OpenAI reasoning models, `reasoning_effort: 'high'`.
271	
272	**Tool use reliability (critical).** The model must call tools with correct schema adherence on every turn. Incorrect parameter types, missing required fields, or hallucinated tool names cause the executor to return errors, wasting turns. Models with strong native function-calling (not prompting-based tool use) are required. Tool-call accuracy should be tested explicitly during model selection — a model with 95% per-call accuracy fails 40% of 8-turn investigations.
273	
274	**Long-context coherence (important).** By turn 5–6 the context may be 20,000–40,000 tokens: the stable prefix, the full evidence slice, 4–5 prior turns of tool calls and results. The model must stay coherent over this context — remembering what it already called, what the results were, and what hypothesis it is currently testing. Models that lose track of their own earlier tool results and re-call the same tools waste turns.
275	
276	**Calibrated self-knowledge (important).** The model needs to know what it does and does not know. Over-confident models produce definitive diagnoses from insufficient evidence; under-confident ones never reach a verdict and exhaust the turn cap hedging. The ideal behaviour is explicit: "my hypothesis is X at 85% confidence; the next tool call would confirm or rule it out; if the result is Y the answer is X, if Z I need to investigate further". Models that express well-calibrated uncertainty in their reasoning produce better stopping decisions.
277	
278	**Structured output fidelity (important).** Every turn must produce a valid JSON object matching the output schema — hypothesis, confidence, decision type, and the relevant payload. The schema is non-trivial (nested, typed, conditional fields). Models that frequently produce malformed JSON or fill optional fields inconsistently require extra retry/repair logic that wastes turns and tokens.
279	
280	**Instruction following under constraint (important).** The system prompt contains hard rules: never execute write commands, always propose tier-2/3 actions for approval, aggregate before paginating, do not re-chew evidence you already have. Models that reliably follow complex multi-rule instruction sets under the pressure of an active investigation (where the "obvious" next move might violate a rule) are strongly preferred.
281	
282	**Domain familiarity with Linux storage and Synology DSM (helpful but compensatable).** The NAS taxonomy section of the prompt provides the known issue families and DSM blind spots explicitly. A strong general reasoner can work from this. However, a model with pre-training familiarity with `/proc/mdstat` format, BTRFS device status output, and Synology's log structure will recognise signal in tool output faster and make better tool-selection decisions. This is a secondary factor — a model that is weaker here but stronger on reasoning and tool use will outperform a domain-familiar model with poor multi-step reasoning.
283	
284	**Properties that are NOT needed for this stage:**
285	- Creative writing or natural-language fluency — the output is JSON and a hypothesis string, not prose
286	- Large output generation — most turns produce 200–600 tokens of output; output length is not a bottleneck
287	- Web search or retrieval — the tool layer handles all data access; the model does not need to retrieve external knowledge
288	- Speed-optimised / low-latency serving — this is a background worker; a 30-second model call per turn is acceptable; correctness matters far more than throughput
289	
290	**Recommended model tier:** A frontier reasoning model. Anthropic Claude Opus / Sonnet with extended thinking, OpenAI `o-series` with `reasoning_effort: high`, or Google Gemini with thinking config enabled. Do not use a fast/small model for this stage — the turn cap is 8, so the total cost is bounded, and a weak model burning through all 8 turns without reaching a diagnosis is more expensive than a strong model converging in 3.
291	
292	---
293	
294	### §3.3 Stage 3 — Explainer / Memory (cheap model, single-shot)
295	
296	#### What this stage is doing
297	
298	Stage 3 runs once, after Stage 2 reaches a terminal decision (`resolved` or `stuck`). It has two outputs with different audiences and different purposes:
299	
300	**Output 1: Operator message.** A 2–5 sentence plain-language summary written for the owner of the NAS — a non-developer who understands that things broke but not why. The message must translate the investigation's technical findings into human terms: what happened, what caused it, what was done or proposed, and what to watch for next. The operator message is posted to `issue_messages` with `role="agent"` and appears in the dashboard as the final agent response.
301	
302	**Output 2: Memory entries.** Up to 5 durable `agent_memory` records extracted from this issue's investigation. Each entry must be specific, non-obvious, and genuinely reusable — it should capture something that would meaningfully improve Stage 2's reasoning on a future issue of the same type. Generic observations ("ShareSync can fail") are not memory. Actionable specifics are ("edgesynology1's ShareSync metadata DB becomes corrupted after a hard power cycle; the symptom is error code 2006 in the sync log, not a generic sync-fail alert; the fix is a DB repair via the DSM package manager, not a restart").
303	
304	The operator message and memory entries are generated in a single model call with a structured JSON output schema. Stage 3 is wrapped in `try/catch` in `pipeline-v2` — a Stage 3 failure must never fail or undo the issue resolution that Stage 2 already reached.
305	
306	#### Context fed to Stage 3
307	
308	- Issue fields: `title`, `severity`, `status`, `affected_nas`, `current_hypothesis`, `conversation_summary`
309	- Evidence highlights: `issue_evidence_items` filtered to `in_scope=true`, most recent 30 rows, body truncated to 300 chars each — enough for Stage 3 to ground the operator message in specifics without the full evidence set
310	- Action history: `issue_actions`, first 20 rows — what was actually done or proposed
311	
312	Stage 3 does not receive the full multi-turn Stage 2 transcript. It receives the outcome and the curated highlights. The intent is that Stage 2 has already done the diagnostic work; Stage 3 is a communication and distillation layer, not a re-investigation.
313	
314	#### Memory types (max 5 per issue)
315	
316	Each memory entry must be assigned a type that describes what kind of knowledge it encodes:
317	
318	| Type | What it records | Example |
319	|---|---|---|
320	| `nas_profile` | Persistent hardware or software characteristics of a specific named NAS unit that affect how to interpret its telemetry or how to interact with it | "edgesynology2's BTRFS scrub reliably triggers iowait spikes above 60% for the first 4–6 hours; this is normal for its disk configuration and should not be treated as a storage emergency unless accompanied by device errors" |
321	| `issue_pattern` | A recurring failure pattern that has a recognisable symptom signature and a known effective response | "ShareSync metadata corruption on either NAS always presents as error 2006 in the syncfolder log, not as a generic sync-fail alert, and persists through restarts until the DB is repaired; the repair procedure is X" |
322	| `calibration` | A threshold, baseline, or expected-value insight for a specific metric or alert type on this system | "The Hyper Backup job for the primary volume takes 4–6 hours on Sunday nights and routinely generates iowait warnings above the 20% threshold; these warnings during that window are not actionable" |
323	| `institutional` | Human-facing process, ownership, or escalation knowledge that the agent cannot derive from telemetry | "The owner does not want the Synology Drive package restarted during business hours (8am–6pm UTC+8) because client sync sessions are active; always propose an off-hours maintenance window for Drive package restarts" |
324	
325	#### What model capabilities this stage requires — and which matter most
326	
327	Stage 3 has almost opposite requirements from Stage 2. The task is communication and pattern distillation, not investigation. The model capabilities that matter are:
328	
329	**Writing quality and register calibration (critical).** The operator message must be clear, jargon-free, and calibrated to a non-developer reader. The model must translate "BTRFS device had 3 uncorrectable read errors on /dev/sdb3 which caused ShareSync to abort with error 2006" into language the owner understands, without being condescending or losing the essential fact. Models with strong natural language output quality matter here; a model that produces technically accurate but stiff, jargon-heavy prose fails at the actual goal.
330	
331	**Synthesis under compression (important).** Stage 3 receives a rich investigation transcript and must produce 2–5 sentences. Selecting which facts belong in those sentences — and which to omit without losing the essential meaning — is a non-trivial compression task. Models that reliably identify the most relevant causal chain and anchor the summary to it, rather than producing a generic "there was an issue and it was investigated" summary, are strongly preferred.
332	
333	**Pattern extraction quality (important).** Memory entries are only useful if they are specific and durable. The model must distinguish between observations that are generalisable (and worth encoding as memory) and observations that are specific to this one incident's state at a point in time (which should not be encoded). A model that produces generic memory entries is producing noise. The quality test for a memory entry: would a fresh Stage 2 turn on a future issue of the same type make a materially better decision if it had this memory, versus not having it?
334	
335	**Structured output fidelity (important).** The output schema includes both the operator message string and a typed array of memory entries. The model must produce valid JSON, correctly typed, with the right number of entries. The failure mode is malformed output or producing more than 5 memory entries of poor specificity.
336	
337	**Properties that are NOT needed for this stage:**
338	
339	- Multi-step reasoning or extended thinking — there is no hypothesis to test, no tool to call, no decision tree to navigate. The investigation is over; this stage just communicates it.
340	- Tool use — Stage 3 makes no tool calls. The `try/catch` wrapper exists precisely so that even if this stage fails completely, nothing breaks.
341	- Long-context coherence across turns — this is a single-shot call with a bounded, carefully selected context. The model does not need to track state across multiple turns.
342	- Domain expertise in Linux/storage — the evidence highlights provide the grounded facts; the model is translating them, not interpreting them.
343	- Reasoning effort controls — do not waste `extended_thinking` budget or `reasoning_effort: high` tokens here. The task does not benefit from deeper reasoning; it benefits from better writing.
344	
345	**Recommended model tier:** A capable but fast and inexpensive model. Claude Haiku, GPT-4o-mini, Gemini Flash, or similar. The operator message quality difference between a frontier model and a good mid-tier model is small for this task; the cost and latency difference is large. Because Stage 3 is wrapped in `try/catch` and its failure is non-fatal, a cheaper model with occasional output quality variation is an acceptable tradeoff.
346

365	## 5. Context budget & evidence retrieval (resolves the "lossless vs. window" tension)
366	
367	"Lossless" applies to the **persisted evidence store**, not to a single prompt. A
368	model context is bounded and priced per token regardless of how cheap disk is.
369	
370	- **Persist, don't inline.** Stage 1 writes the full deduped evidence set to the DB
371	  (extend `issue_evidence`/`facts`, or a new `issue_evidence_items` table keyed by
372	  `issue_id` with `(source, ts, severity, body, dedup_count)`). Nothing distinct is
373	  dropped.
374	- **Bounded slice into Stage 2.** Stage 2's prompt carries only a prioritized,
375	  budgeted slice: (a) all in-scope + anomalous events in full, (b) dedup-with-counts
376	  summaries for high-volume noise, (c) an **index** of what else exists (source ×
377	  time-bucket × count). Define an explicit `EVIDENCE_TOKEN_BUDGET` for this block.
378	- **Retrieval-by-ID for the rest — bounded, aggregable, paginated.** Provide a
379	  `fetch_evidence` tool (§6) so Stage 2 pulls more detail on demand instead of
380	  pre-loading it (the same mechanism as live NAS tools — the agent decides what it
381	  needs, like an SSH session). The schema MUST be safe against a cascade that
382	  produces tens of thousands of rows:
383	  - **server-side hard caps** — `limit` is clamped to a max (e.g. 100) *regardless*
384	    of what the model requests, and a bounded `start_time`/`end_time` is required;
385	  - **byte cap on the result, not just rows** — one pathological log line can blow
386	    the budget at a single row, so cap total result bytes and truncate row bodies
387	    in the *result*;
388	  - **a cursor** — return `has_more` + `next_offset` + total match count so the
389	    model knows more exists (never silent truncation) and can paginate;
390	  - **aggregation mode** — support count/group-by queries (`group_by: source |
391	    error | time_bucket`) so the agent sees the *shape* of 50k lines cheaply
392	    instead of brute-forcing through them. This is what keeps the cap from blunting
393	    effectiveness: an expert greps-and-counts rather than `cat`-ing everything, and
394	    a model can't reason over 50k raw lines in one prompt anyway. The prompt
395	    instructs the model to aggregate first, then page into specifics.
396	- **Overflow rule.** If even the prioritized in-scope set exceeds the budget,
397	  include the index + a representative sample and rely on `fetch_evidence`; `log()`
398	  / record that truncation happened so it's visible, never silent.
399	- **Keep the cached prefix lean.** Evidence is **dynamic** (per-issue, per-turn) and
400	  belongs in the dynamic suffix, never in the cached prefix (§9). The cacheable
401	  region is system + tool schemas + output schema + taxonomy; the whole-system
402	  snapshot is semi-stable (cache within an issue).

406	## 6. Tool integration boundary (decision, not an option)
407	
408	Stage 2 needs the curated catalog, which is *machinery* — lazy discovery,
409	`tools-config.json` gating, approval previews, tuned timeouts — not a command list
410	(`apps/nas-mcp` README + `architecture.md`).
411	
412	**Decision: share the definitions, execute in the web app. Do NOT route Stage 2
413	through nas-mcp.**
414	
415	- **Single source of tool definitions** → move them into `packages/shared` (name,
416	  tier, params/schema, group, `buildCommand`, enable flag). Both `apps/nas-mcp` and
417	  `apps/web` import from there. This removes the current duplication
418	  (`nas-mcp/tool-definitions.ts` ~108 vs `web/.../tools.ts` ~42); the cost is one
419	  shared module to keep in sync, which is the point of the package.
420	- **Stage 2 executes directly** through the web app's existing `nas-api-client.ts`
421	  + approval/HMAC/tier machinery (`buildApprovalToken`, tier preview). It does
422	  **not** call nas-mcp's `tool_search`/`invoke_tool` lazy-load surface — that
423	  indirection exists for token-starved chat clients; an autonomous agent should
424	  hold real tool schemas directly (Stage 2's prompt budget is managed in §5/§9, not
425	  by hiding tools).
426	- **Reuse, don't reinvent:** enable/disable gating (a `tools-config`-equivalent),
427	  tier classification + approval preview, and the 8s/25s/45s timeout discipline all
428	  move with the shared definitions or are reused from the web app's NAS client.
429	- **Server-runtime only (enforce by package boundary / lint, not a Next.js
430	  marker).** The shared defs include `buildCommand` shell templates and must be
431	  imported only from server runtimes — `apps/web` *server* code and `apps/nas-mcp`
432	  — never from client components. Because the package is also consumed by a
433	  plain-Node app (nas-mcp), do **not** lean on a Next.js-only `import 'server-only'`
434	  marker; enforce it with package boundaries / lint rules / code review. (Not
435	  secret — they're in the repo — but they have no business in the client bundle.)
436	- `apps/nas-mcp` keeps consuming the same shared definitions for its chat clients;
437	  its lazy-load surface is unchanged (that's an intentional quirk — see AGENTS.md).
438	
439	**Build-system cost of sharing (own it, don't gloss it):** moving the definitions
440	(and any `tools-config`-equivalent gating) into `packages/shared` is not free in
441	this monorepo:
442	- `web-image.yml` already builds from the repo root and already watches
443	  `packages/shared/**` — web is fine.
444	- **`nas-mcp-image.yml` builds with `context: ./apps/nas-mcp`**, so it currently
445	  *cannot see* `packages/shared` at all, and its `paths:` filter does **not**
446	  include `packages/shared/**`. To share, you must (a) change the nas-mcp build
447	  context to the repo root + update `apps/nas-mcp/Dockerfile`, and (b) add
448	  `packages/shared/**` to `nas-mcp-image.yml`'s `paths:` — otherwise a shared-tool
449	  edit silently rebuilds web but **not** nas-mcp, and the config change never
450	  reaches production.
451	- This is **required migration work, not an open architectural question** — the
452	  decision (top of §6) is to share. The build-context move + `paths:` change above
453	  are part of that migration. Sharing removes the current ~108-vs-42 duplication;
454	  the one-time build change is simply its cost.
