458	## 7. Workflow, approval & resume state machine
459	
460	Collapsing four stages into one loop must not break the existing job/approval
461	layer. The process **dies at every approval/user gate** and may resume in a
462	different worker after the cache TTL lapses, so:
463	
464	**Invariant: Stage 2 is resumable from DB state only — never from in-memory loop
465	state or a warm cache.**
466	
467	- **A Stage-2 "turn"** loads issue state, rebuilds the prompt from the persisted
468	  transcript (issue_messages/evidence) + the bounded evidence slice, runs the
469	  model (with tool calls for read-only data), and ends in exactly one terminal:
470	  | Turn outcome | DB effect | Status set | Next |
471	  |---|---|---|---|
472	  | needs tier-2/3 action | persist the action **intent** (tool name + tier + args + command preview) + transcript — **never the HMAC token** | `waiting_for_approval` | return; operator decides |
473	  | needs operator input | persist question + transcript | `waiting_on_user` | return |
474	  | read-only diagnostic only | persist tool results to evidence + transcript | `running` | enqueue next `run_issue` job (bounded by cycle cap) |
475	  | blocked on another issue | persist `depends_on_issue_id` | `waiting_on_issue` (**new** — added below) | `releaseDependentIssues` re-queues |
476	  | done | persist verdict | `resolved` / `stuck` | Stage 3 |
477	- **Approval resume — mint the token at execution time, never persist it.** The
478	  HMAC approval token expires in 15 minutes, but propose→approve can be hours, so a
479	  persisted token would be expired on resume and nas-api would return 403. Persist
480	  only the intent (row above). On approval, an `approval_decision` job is enqueued;
481	  the resumed Stage-2 turn re-enters with the persisted transcript + approved
482	  intent, **mints a fresh HMAC token immediately before the nas-api call**, executes,
483	  then **verifies in the same loop** (the next turn evaluates the result —
484	  verification is not a separate stage). The 15-min window then bounds only
485	  exec→exec (seconds), never the operator's think time.
486	- **NAS unreachable / Tailscale down — degrade, don't hang.** Live tools reach
487	  nas-api only over Tailscale; if the daemon/NAS is down, every call throws
488	  `ECONNREFUSED`. Handle this in **code, not just the prompt**: the tool layer
489	  retries once, then returns a structured `nas_unreachable` result (not a raw
490	  exception), and the whole-system snapshot carries a reachability flag. `fetch_evidence`
491	  reads **Supabase**, not the NAS, so it still works — the agent falls back to
492	  diagnosing from stored telemetry and tells the operator the NAS appears offline.
493	  Operator-facing UX: a periodic `GET /health` probe against nas-api drives an
494	  **Offline Mode** in the dashboard — live-action controls are disabled and an
495	  "NAS offline" badge is shown, so operators aren't left clicking actions that will
496	  fail. (Read-only views and `fetch_evidence`-backed diagnosis stay available.)
497	- **Re-entry safety:** because the transcript is the source of truth, a resumed
498	  turn reconstructs identical context (cache hit if within TTL, cache miss = cost
499	  only, never a correctness change). `hasAlreadyTried` is replaced by the re-chew
500	  guard (§3) operating over the persisted transcript.
501	- Keep `issue_jobs`, the job types, the cycle cap (rename to a turn cap), and the
502	  `stuck` guard. Map the four old stages' status transitions onto the table above.
503	  This introduces **one new status, `waiting_on_issue`** — the current
504	  `issues.status` CHECK allows only the seven in §2 (verified against the DB), so
505	  the workflow migration must add `waiting_on_issue` to that CHECK. (Cross-issue
506	  blocking exists today via `depends_on_issue_id`/`releaseDependentIssues` but
507	  without a dedicated status.)
508

570	## 9. Caching — provider-native, correct, resume-safe
571	
572	Hard-won production lessons (adapted from a system called "Oracle"). Principles +
573	gotchas, not copy-paste. The numeric thresholds are Oracle's tuning — **re-derive
574	against this app's pricing/reuse before trusting them.**
575	
576	### 9.0 This app's answers to the prerequisite questions
577	- **Providers:** Anthropic, OpenAI, Google Gemini, DeepSeek, Qwen/DashScope (direct
578	  keys). **Remove OpenRouter from the inference path.**
579	- **Stable vs dynamic:** *stable* (cache target) = system instructions, tool
580	  schemas, output schema, NAS taxonomy. *Semi-stable* (per-issue) = whole-system
581	  snapshot, issue summary. *Dynamic* (every turn) = the bounded evidence slice
582	  (§5), tool-call results, the per-turn instruction, retry suffix. **Evidence is
583	  dynamic — never in the cached prefix.**
584	- **Single-shot vs multi-turn:** Stage 2 is **multi-turn** (the turn loop) — cache
585	  the prior-turns prefix. Stages 1 & 3 are single-shot but reused across many
586	  issues, so their stable instruction/schema prefix caches across calls.
587	
588	### 9.1 Foundational decisions
589	1. **Provider-native SDKs** (`@anthropic-ai/sdk`, `openai`, `@google/genai`,
590	   DeepSeek's OpenAI-compatible SDK, DashScope) — **no** normalizing abstraction
591	   (no Vercel AI SDK) and **no** aggregator on the inference path. Aggregators erase
592	   per-provider cache usage fields; if you can't see cache reads/writes you can't
593	   tune. (An aggregator is fine for the model-catalog/pricing dropdown only.)
594	   **Wrap vs. flatten — the line that matters:** a thin per-provider client that
595	   standardizes *operational* concerns (timeouts, retries, error classification incl.
596	   `nas_unreachable`) is encouraged — that IS the "provider client" in §10 step 2.
597	   What's forbidden is a unifying layer that flattens the *request/response/usage*
598	   shape: each provider's native usage object and native cache controls
599	   (`cache_control`, retention, session id, `cachedContent`) must pass through
600	   untouched, or cache observability dies. Wrap the plumbing; never normalize the
601	   payload. This belongs in `apps/web`/`packages/shared` (where the agent runs), not
602	   in `apps/nas-mcp`.
603	2. **Stable-before-dynamic — enforced.** Order every prompt `[stable system] →
604	   [stable tools] → [stable schema] → [semi-stable taxonomy] → [whole-system
605	   snapshot] → [bounded evidence] → [dynamic instruction] → [retry]`. Build a
606	   **context compiler** that sorts blocks into this order and **throws** if a
607	   dynamic block precedes a stable one. One early dynamic token busts the cache for
608	   everything after.
609	
610	### 9.2 Per-provider mechanics
611	- **Anthropic** — explicit `cache_control:{type:'ephemeral',ttl:'5m'|'1h'}`. Mark
612	  the last text part of the stable prefix; for Stage 2 mark the **penultimate**
613	  message so the latest turn stays dynamic and prior turns cache. Min cacheable
614	  ~1024 tok (Sonnet) / ~2048 (Haiku) — below that skip it. Usage:
615	  `cache_creation_input_tokens` (write), `cache_read_input_tokens` (read). Max 4
616	  breakpoints; 5m interactive / 1h batch. `temperature` must be 1 with extended
617	  thinking.
618	- **OpenAI** — automatic prefix cache (≥1024 tok); just keep the prefix stable.
619	  Tune `prompt_cache_retention: 'in_memory' | '24h'`. Usage:
620	  `prompt_tokens_details.cached_tokens`.
621	- **Gemini/Vertex** — implicit (`usageMetadata.cachedContentTokenCount`) + explicit
622	  `cachedContent` (billed hourly while alive). Explicit only when big+reused —
623	  Oracle heuristic `(≥25k tok AND ≥3 reuses) OR (≥100k AND ≥2)`; re-derive. Server
624	  min TTL 60s; 1h chat / 24h batch.
625	- **DeepSeek** — automatic disk-backed prefix cache; usage is DeepSeek-specific
626	  (`prompt_cache_hit_tokens` / `prompt_cache_miss_tokens`, **not** the OpenAI
627	  shape).
628	- **Qwen/DashScope** — OpenAI-style `cache_control` markers; for multi-turn, persist
629	  `previous_response_id` (without it the session cache resets every turn).
630	
631	### 9.3 Breakpoints per stage
632	- **Stage 1 / 3 (single-shot, reused):** stable instructions+schema prefix first;
633	  Anthropic one breakpoint at its end (if ≥ min size); OpenAI/DeepSeek automatic.
634	- **Stage 2 (multi-turn):** stable+semi-stable region is the cacheable prefix;
635	  Anthropic breakpoint on the penultimate message; OpenAI/DeepSeek automatic; Qwen
636	  persist `previous_response_id`.
637	
638	### 9.4 Cache correctness under resume (ties to §7)
639	The cache is **never** load-bearing. Stage 2 rebuilds its prompt from the persisted
640	transcript on every turn/resume; a hit saves money, a miss costs money, neither
641	changes behavior. Do not store conversation state only in a provider session — a
642	resumed turn (new process, expired TTL) must reconstruct identical context from the
643	DB. Derive a `stable_prefix_hash` from the stable blocks for reuse keys; the
644	stable-before-dynamic ordering is what makes that hash meaningful.
645	
646	### 9.5 Observability & explicit-cache lifecycle
647	- Normalize every provider's usage into one struct `{ inputTokens, outputTokens,
648	  cachedInputTokens, cacheWriteTokens, reasoningTokens }` with a per-provider
649	  normalizer (incl. DeepSeek's + Gemini's non-standard fields); persist normalized
650	  **and** raw usage. Track `cache_hit_ratio = cachedInputTokens / inputTokens` — an
651	  un-normalized provider silently reads 0%.
652	- If explicit Gemini caches are used, track them in a `provider_cached_content`
653	  table (`source_hash`, `expected_reuse_count`, `hard_expiration_at`,
654	  `cleanup_owner`, `status`); status changes via a `recordCacheTermination()`
655	  helper; CHECK constraint `deleted_at IS NULL iff status='active'`; delete in a
656	  `finally` when no follow-up. In-memory tracking leaks billing across worker/web
657	  processes.
658	
659	### 9.6 Mistakes not to repeat
660	Normalizing SDK → lost observability. A doc-cache optimization collapsed multi-turn
661	history → broke conversation. Process-local cache tracking → leaked caches. Qwen
662	session id not persisted → cache reset each turn. Un-normalized usage field → 0%
663	dashboard.
664	
665	### 9.7 CI guards (silent failures)
666	Hard blockers in the build command: (1) stable-before-dynamic ordering (the context
667	compiler throws); (2) Stage-2 multi-turn history is preserved through any caching
668	path; (3) every provider's cache usage field is normalized.

1	# AI Operating Rules
2	
3	## Purpose
4	
5	These rules exist so AI tools can safely assist with this repo without creating production drift.
6	
7	## System of truth
8	
9	- GitHub is the source of truth for code, Docker Compose, Dockerfiles, and workflows.
10	- Coolify is the source of truth for production runtime environment variables and deployment target settings.
11	- The production server is only a runtime host, not a configuration source.
12	
13	## Branch policy
14	
15	- This repo uses one branch only: `main`
16	- Do not propose or create feature branches
17	- Do not suggest branch-based workflows
18	- Do not assume there is a staging branch
19	- All approved changes should target `main`
20	
21	## Approved deployment path
22	
23	The only normal deployment path is:
24	
25	1. change files in this repo
26	2. commit to `main`
27	3. GitHub Actions builds and pushes the image to GHCR
28	
29	For `apps/web` and `apps/nas-mcp`:
30	
31	4. GitHub Actions calls the Coolify webhook
32	5. Coolify redeploys automatically
33	
34	For `apps/agent` and `apps/nas-api`:
35	
36	4. Watchtower on each NAS detects the new image and automatically recreates the containers (every 5 min poll)
37	
38	Do not propose alternate routine deployment methods.
39	
40	## Allowed AI actions
41	
42	AI may help with:
43	
44	- editing application code
45	- editing `docker-compose.yml`
46	- editing Dockerfiles
47	- editing GitHub Actions workflows
48	- editing documentation
49	- recommending GitHub Secrets usage for CI/CD
50	- applying Coolify runtime environment variable changes directly through the Coolify API or UI (env vars, feature flags, health checks, restart policy, domain bindings — settings Coolify owns)
51	- triggering deployment through the approved GitHub -> Coolify path
52	
53	## Forbidden AI actions
54	
55	AI must not:
56	
57	- use SSH as the normal deployment path
58	- hand-edit files directly on the production server
59	- assume the server contains the source of truth
60	- create undocumented hotfixes on the live machine
61	- introduce additional branches
62	- create a second deployment system
63	- recommend storing production runtime configuration only in ad hoc server files
64	
65	## Secrets rule
66	
67	- GitHub Secrets are for CI/CD and build-time secrets
68	- Coolify stores production runtime environment variables
69	- Do not move all runtime secrets into GitHub if the running app is managed by Coolify
70	
71	## Compose rule
72	
73	- The repo copy of `docker-compose.yml` is authoritative
74	- If a service exists, it should be declared in the repo
75	- Do not assume server-side Compose changes are valid unless they are committed
76	
77	## Change discipline
78	
79	When making changes:
80	
81	- prefer small, explicit edits
82	- preserve the single-branch workflow
83	- keep deployment logic simple
84	- avoid introducing tools or processes that require manual server babysitting
85	
86	## Decision preference
87	
88	When multiple valid options exist, prefer the option that:
89	
90	- keeps `main` as the single source of truth
91	- keeps production behavior reproducible
92	- reduces hidden state on the server
93	- is easier for a non-developer owner to understand and audit
94

1	# Architecture
2	
3	## System purpose
4	
5	Monitors two Synology NAS devices (`edgesynology1`, `edgesynology2`) and gives the operator:
6	
7	- live telemetry (metrics, logs, storage, processes, network, containers)
8	- grouped issues with persistent conversation threads
9	- a 3-stage LLM-driven issue agent that diagnoses problems and proposes fixes
10	- a controlled approval gate for any state-modifying action on the NAS
11	
12	Focus is Synology Drive / ShareSync reliability, file operation visibility, sync
13	and replication failures, storage and I/O attribution, and silent task/backup
14	failures.
15	
16	## Component map
17	
18	```
19	┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
20	│ NAS (×2: edgesynology1, edgesynology2)                      │
21	│  ┌──────────────────────┐  ┌────────────────────────────┐   │
22	│  │  agent (Go)          │  │  nas-api (Go)              │   │
23	│  │  polls DSM APIs      │  │  3-tier shell executor     │   │
24	│  │  reads /proc, /sys   │  │  port 7734                 │   │
25	│  │  watches logs        │  │  tier 1: read-only (auto)  │   │
26	│  │  → SQLite WAL        │  │  tier 2: service ops       │   │
27	│  └──────────────────────┘  │  tier 3: file ops          │   │
28	│           │                 └────────────┬───────────────┘   │
29	│           │ PostgREST (batch flush)       │ HTTPS/Tailscale   │
30	└───────────┼───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────┘
31	            ▼                               ▼
32	┌───────────────────┐    ┌───────────────────────────────────┐
33	│ Supabase          │◄───│ web app (Next.js)                  │
34	│ qnjimovrsaacneqk  │    │ mon.designflow.app                 │
35	│ 53 tables         │    │  - issue detector                  │
36	│ partitioned +     │    │  - 3-stage issue-agent pipeline    │
37	│ time-series       │    │  - operator approval UI            │
38	└───────────────────┘    │  - copilot chat                    │
39	                         └───────────────────────────────────┘
40	                                        ▲
41	                              MCP / Streamable HTTP
42	                         ┌───────────────────────────────────┐
43	                         │ nas-mcp (Node.js)                  │
44	                         │ nas-mcp.designflow.app/mcp         │
45	                         │ 119-definition registry (lazy-load)      │
46	                         │ 5 always-on tools per session      │
47	                         └───────────────────────────────────┘
48	```
49	
50	Everything is deployed via push to `main`. GitHub Actions builds and pushes four
51	Docker images to GHCR. The web app and nas-mcp redeploy automatically via Coolify
52	webhooks; the agent and nas-api are picked up by Watchtower on each NAS within
53	~5 minutes. There is one branch: `main`.
54	
55	---
56	
57	## Agent (`apps/agent`)
58	
59	The Go agent runs on each NAS as a Docker container with `network_mode: host`.
60	It collects telemetry on a rolling schedule, buffers every write in a local SQLite
61	WAL at `/app/data/wal.db`, and flushes to Supabase in per-table batches every 30s
62	via PostgREST.
63	
64	### Volume mounts
65	
66	The NAS host filesystem is exposed to the agent container under `/host/` to avoid
67	shadowing the container's own kernel namespaces:
68	
69	| Host path | Container path | Used by |
70	|---|---|---|
71	| `/proc` | `/host/proc` | process stats, mdstat, diskstats |
72	| `/sys` | `/host/sys` | cgroup I/O, btrfs counters, thermal |
73	| `/var/log` | `/host/log` | logwatcher, backup logs |
74	| `/volume1/@synologydrive` | `/host/shares/@synologydrive` | drive log collector |
75	| `/volume1/@SynologyDriveShareSync` | `/host/shares/@SynologyDriveShareSync` | sharesync log collector |
76	
77	### Collector inventory
78	
79	| Collector | File | Primary tables | Default interval |
80	|---|---|---|---|
81	| system | `collector/system.go` | `metrics`, `container_status`, `storage_snapshots` | 30s / 60s |
82	| drive | `collector/drive.go` | `sync_task_snapshots`, `drive_team_folders`, `nas_logs` | 30s |
83	| sharesync | `collector/sharesync.go` | `alerts`, `nas_logs` | 5m (hardcoded) |
84	| sharehealth | `collector/sharehealth.go` | `nas_logs`, `metrics`, `package_status`, `dsm_errors` | 2m |
85	| storagepool | `collector/storagepool.go` | `snapshot_replicas`, `metrics`, `nas_logs` | 60s (mdstat) / 5m (replicas) |
86	| process | `collector/process.go` | `process_snapshots` | 15s |
87	| diskstats | `collector/diskstats.go` | `disk_io_stats`, `metrics` (`disk_inflight_ios`) | 15s |
88	| container_io | `collector/container_io.go` | `container_io` | 30s |
89	| connections | `collector/connections.go` | `net_connections` | 30s |
90	| sysextras | `collector/sysextras.go` | `metrics` (cpu_iowait_pct, thermal, memory, NFS, VM pressure, btrfs errors) | 30s |
91	| services | `collector/services.go` | `service_health`, `nas_logs` | 60s |
92	| hyperbackup | `collector/hyperbackup.go` | `backup_tasks`, `nas_logs` | 5m |
93	| schedtasks | `collector/schedtasks.go` | `scheduled_tasks`, `nas_logs` | 5m |
94	| infra | `collector/infra.go` | `metrics`, `nas_logs` | 2m |
95	| custom | `collector/custom.go` | `custom_metric_data` | polls DB every 60s |
96	| logwatcher | `logwatcher/watcher.go` | `nas_logs` | 10s tail interval |
97	| security | `security/watcher.go` | `security_events` | inotify-driven |
98	
99	All collector goroutines use the `wg.Add(1)` / `defer wg.Done()` WaitGroup
100	pattern in `cmd/agent/main.go`. Omitting this drops in-flight WAL writes on
101	shutdown (the ShareSync collector had this bug; see AGENTS.md §10).
102	
103	### Notable collectors
104	
105	**ShareSync health (`collector/sharesync.go`)** — scans `dscc.log` and
106	`dscc_monitor.log` directly at
107	`/host/shares/@SynologyDriveShareSync/log/` using byte-offset watermarks stored
108	in the WAL's `checkpoints` table. Detects three failure patterns invisible to the
109	DSM UI: queue jams (same path in `RedoEvent`/`PullEvent` ≥5 times without a
110	`DoneEvent`), basis-file corruption (`PrepareDownloadFile` repeat without
111	`DoneEvent`), and transport flaps (≥3 error-code-26 / daemon-socket / reconnect
112	events in a window).
113	
114	**Storagepool (`collector/storagepool.go`)** — reads `/host/proc/mdstat` directly
115	every 60s for RAID scrub/rebuild/check progress. Also emits `disk_inflight_ios`
116	(instantaneous in-progress I/O count per device from `/proc/diskstats` field 9).
117	Alerts once on healthy→degraded state transitions.
118	
119	**Log watcher (`logwatcher/watcher.go`)** — tails the following sources by default:
120	
121	| Container path | Source label |
122	|---|---|
123	| `/host/log/messages` | `system` |
124	| `/host/log/synolog/synobackup.log` | `backup` |
125	| `/host/log/synologydrive.log` | `drive_server` |
126	| `/host/log/synolog/synosecurity.log` | `security` |
127	| `/host/log/synolog/synoconnection.log` | `connection` |
128	| `/host/log/synolog/synopkg.log` | `package` |
129	| `/host/log/samba/log.smbd` | `smb` |
130	| `/host/log/synolog/synowebapi.log` | `webapi` |
131	| `/host/log/synolog/synostorage.log` | `storage` |
132	| `/host/log/synolog/synoshare.log` | `share` |
133	| `/host/log/kern.log` | `kernel` |
134	| `/host/log/synolog/synoinfo.log` | `system_info` |
135	| `/host/log/synolog/synoservice.log` | `service` |
136	| `/host/shares/@synologydrive/log/*.log` | `drive` |
137	| `/host/shares/@synologydrive/log/syncfolder.log` | `drive_sharesync` |
138	
139	The `@synologydrive` paths are mounted at `/host/shares/@synologydrive` (not under
140	`/host/volume1`); `inferDriveLogFiles` prepends `/host/shares` first.
141	
142	**Custom collector (`collector/custom.go`)** — polls `custom_metric_schedules`
143	every 60s, claims due rows with an optimistic lock, and executes the scheduled
144	shell command. Output is stored in `custom_metric_data`. Used for ad-hoc deep
145	diagnostics and permanent nightly schedules (e.g. `nightly_disk_health` from
146	migration 00040, which runs at 2am UTC on weekdays and collects `/proc/diskstats`,
147	`/proc/mdstat`, and per-device inflight IOs).
148	
149	### WAL and sender (`sender/sender.go`)
150	
151	The SQLite WAL at `/app/data/wal.db` holds two logical stores: per-table row
152	queues (flushed to Supabase every 30s) and a `checkpoints` table (durable named
153	string values such as log file byte offsets and collector cursors).
154	
155	**Poison-row isolation:** on a PostgREST `4xx` batch failure, the sender
156	re-sends each row individually so good rows land and only the bad row accumulates
157	retries. This prevents a single malformed row from silently freezing an entire
158	telemetry stream (as happened for ~19h/23d before the fix — see incidents).
159	
160	**`package_status` is the only merge-duplicates upsert.** Every other table is
161	append-only inserts. The WAL is capped at `MAX_WAL_SIZE_MB` (default 100 MB);
162	oldest entries are dropped when the cap is reached.
163	
164	---
165	
166	## NAS API (`apps/nas-api`)
167	
168	Runs on each NAS as a Docker container on port 7734 (`network_mode: host`,
169	`pid: host`). Accepts bearer-authenticated POST requests from the web app and
170	nas-mcp to classify and execute shell commands.
171	
172	The service exposes three HTTP endpoints:
173	
174	| Method + Path | Purpose |
175	|---|---|
176	| `GET /health` | Liveness check; returns build SHA and time |
177	| `POST /preview` | Classify a command's tier without executing it |
178	| `POST /exec` | Execute a command; requires tier + optional approval token |
179	
180	### Three-tier execution model
181	
182	| Tier | Label | Approval required | Examples |
183	|---|---|---|---|
184	| 1 | read-only | No — auto-executes | `cat /proc/mdstat`, `df -h`, `smartctl -a`, `tail -n 100 /var/log/kern.log` |
185	| 2 | service ops | HMAC approval token | `synopkg restart SynologyDrive`, `docker compose restart` |
186	| 3 | file ops | HMAC approval token | `mv /volume1/file.old /volume1/file`, `btrfs scrub start /volume1` |
187	
188	### Validator (`internal/validator/validator.go`)
189	
190	`ClassifyTier(command)` returns the minimum required tier. `Validate(command, tier)`
191	enforces that the requested tier is sufficient and that no hard-block patterns match.
192	
193	**Hard-blocked regardless of tier:** disk destruction (`mkfs`, `fdisk`, `parted`,
194	`dd if=`, `wipefs`), root filesystem destruction (`rm -rf /`), DSM binary writes,
195	`synopkg install/uninstall`, firmware writes (`flash_eraseall`, `nandwrite`,
196	`insmod`, `rmmod`), ptrace code-injection (`gdb`, `lldb`), destructive `hdparm`
197	flags (`--security-`, `-y`, `-Y`, `-W`), user account changes (`useradd`,
198	`userdel`, `passwd <user>`), shutdown/reboot/poweroff, package managers
199	(`apt-get install`, `opkg install`, `pip install`), recursive grep on
200	`@synologydrive`/`@SynologyDriveShareSync` (a 4-day runaway grep incident on
201	production triggered this — see AGENTS.md §13), unrestricted `docker run/create`
202	(only the monitor-stack compose subset is allowed).
203	
204	**Tier 1 enforcement:** `writePatterns` (regex list) is checked; any match elevates
205	to tier 2+. Real output redirection (`> file`, not `>/dev/null` or `2>&1`) is also
206	a write.
207	
208	The validator is compiled by Go's standard `regexp` package, so every regex must
209	be valid RE2 syntax. Lookahead, lookbehind, and backreferences are not supported.
210	Do not use patterns such as `(?!...)` in `regexp.MustCompile`; an invalid pattern
211	panics at process startup and prevents the NAS API from binding port 7734.
212	
213	**Tier 2 enforcement:** commands touching `/volume*/` paths outside the monitor
214	stack are elevated to tier 3.
215	
216	**HMAC approval tokens** are minted fresh by the web app at execution time in
217	`pipeline-v2.ts::executeApprovedAction` and `nas-api-client.ts::buildNasApiApprovalToken`.
218	They are never persisted; the 15-minute expiry bounds only the exec→exec window,
219	not the operator's think time. Persisting tokens would cause 403s after expiry.
220	
221	**Container capabilities:** `apparmor=unconfined` (DSM rejects the default AppArmor
222	profile on container init), `SYS_ADMIN` (required for btrfs subvolume, scrub, and
223	snapshot operations), `SYS_PTRACE` (enables `strace` for D-state process diagnosis;
224	`gdb`/`lldb` are hard-blocked by the validator to prevent code injection).
225	
226	**Executor process group kill:** `executor.go` sets `Setpgid: true` and uses
227	`syscall.Kill(-pid, SIGKILL)` with a `WaitDelay: 2s`. This kills the entire process
228	group on timeout, not just the bash parent — preventing orphaned subprocesses
229	(e.g. a `grep` forked by `grep ... | head`).
230	
231	---
232	
233	## NAS MCP (`apps/nas-mcp`)
234	
235	Node.js MCP server at `nas-mcp.designflow.app/mcp`. AI chat clients (claude.ai,
236	Claude Desktop) connect over Streamable HTTP. The server is **fully stateless** —
237	every request builds a new `McpServer`, handles the request, and discards it.
238	No `mcp-session-id` is tracked. This eliminates stale-session 404s after redeploys
239	and the 4-minute hang that stateful transport caused via the claude.ai proxy.
240	
241	### Tool surface
242	
243	Five tools are registered eagerly on every request (`EAGER_TOOLS` in `src/index.ts`):
244	
245	| Always-on tool | Purpose |
246	|---|---|
247	| `tool_search({ query, limit })` | Search the 119-definition registry by keyword; returns names, descriptions, and parameter shapes as text |
248	| `invoke_tool({ name, target, args })` | Execute any registry tool by name |
249	| `run_command({ target, command })` | Free-form tier-1-only shell command |
250	| `check_disk_space({ target })` | Disk and inode usage across all volumes |
251	| `restart_nas_api({ target, confirmed })` | Restart the NAS API container |
252	
253	The full 119-definition registry is in `packages/shared/src/nas-tools.ts` (the
254	`ALL_TOOL_DEFS` array). Clients discover tools with `tool_search` and execute them
255	with `invoke_tool`. The registry is never loaded eagerly — loading all 119 schemas
256	put ~50k tokens into every session and degraded it after ~10–15 tool calls.
257	`restart_nas_api` is a special always-on MCP tool implemented in
258	`apps/nas-mcp/src/index.ts`, so `tools-config.json` can have 120 enabled entries
259	while `ALL_TOOL_DEFS` has 119 definitions.
260	
261	Tool enablement is controlled by `apps/nas-mcp/tools-config.json`. A tool present
262	in `ALL_TOOL_DEFS` but absent from `enabled_read_tools` or `enabled_write_tools` is
263	rejected by `invoke_tool` with a "disabled" message. Adding a new always-on tool
264	requires adding it to the `EAGER_TOOLS` constant in `src/index.ts`.
265	
266	### NAS API filesystem view used by tools
267	
268	Predefined NAS tools run inside the `synology-monitor-nas-api` container, not in
269	the telemetry agent container. The most important mounts from
270	`deploy/synology/docker-compose.agent.yml` are:
271	
272	| Host path | NAS API path | Notes |
273	|---|---|---|
274	| `/usr/syno` | `/host/usr/syno` | DSM binaries/config |
275	| `/var/packages` | `/host/packages` | DSM package state; not `/host/var/packages` |
276	| `/var/log` | `/host/log` | DSM logs |
277	| `/volume1` | `/btrfs/volume1` | Full Btrfs volume for subvolume/snapshot commands |
278	| selected shares | `/volume1/<share>` | Narrow read-only shared-folder mounts |
279	
280	This is why snapshot tools check `/btrfs/volumeN`, and DSM/package inspectors check
281	`/host/packages`. Read-only tools may also use DSM WebAPI `list`/`query` methods
282	when SQLite/config files are hidden by DSM 7 package layout.
283	
284	### Approval flow for write tools
285	
286	For tier-2/3 write tools, `invoke_tool` calls `POST /preview` on the NAS API first.
287	If the command requires approval and `args.confirmed` is not `true`, the tool
288	returns a plain-text preview of the command. The client must call `invoke_tool`
289	again with `confirmed: true`. On confirmation, `src/index.ts::buildApprovalToken`
290	mints the HMAC token and passes it to `POST /exec`.
291	
292	### Network and timeout architecture
293	
294	- `Connection: close` on every nas-api request — prevents undici pool exhaustion
295	  when timed-out requests do not return their socket (sub-ms Tailscale RTT makes
296	  re-handshake cost negligible).
297	- Node HTTP server: `keepAliveTimeout: 120s` / `headersTimeout: 125s` — above
298	  Traefik's 90s idle timeout so Traefik never reuses a socket Node has already
299	  closed.
300	- Tool deadline: 45s hard cap per `invoke_tool` call, implemented in
301	  `withToolDeadline`. Returns a clear timeout error rather than holding the
302	  connection until Claude's 4-minute client timeout.
303	- `/exec` AbortController: 25s command + 5s buffer = 30s, with `timeout_ms: 25000`
304	  sent to the NAS API so it kills the subprocess first.
305	
306	---
307	
308	## Web app (`apps/web`)
309	
310	Next.js 14 app at `mon.designflow.app`. Uses Supabase for auth and data. Server
311	components and API routes call Supabase with the service role key via
312	`createAdminClient()`. Client-side components use the anon key.
313	
314	`NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL` and `NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY` are baked into the
315	client bundle at `docker build` time via build args in `web-image.yml`. Changing
316	them in Coolify after the image is built has no effect.
317	
318	Key subsystems:
319	
320	| File | Purpose |
321	|---|---|
322	| `lib/server/issue-detector.ts` | Fingerprints alerts + logs into issues by family |
323	| `lib/server/ai/pipeline-v2.ts` | 3-stage pipeline orchestrator, one job per turn |
324	| `lib/server/issue-workflow.ts` | Job queue and status state machine |
325	| `lib/server/issue-agent.ts` | `gatherTelemetryContext` — queries 13+ tables in parallel |
326	| `lib/server/nas-api-client.ts` | HTTP client for nas-api; mints HMAC tokens |
327	| `lib/server/ai-settings.ts` | Model selection + fallback chain from `ai_settings` table |
328	| `lib/server/copilot.ts` | Legacy NAS assistant chat (OpenRouter/MiniMax) |
329	
330	The issue worker mode is controlled by `ISSUE_WORKER_MODE` (`inline` = drains on
331	API requests; `background` = dedicated loop). The worker polls `issue_jobs` and
332	calls `runIssueAgentV2` for each pending job.

334	---
335	
336	## 3-stage AI issue-agent pipeline
337	
338	The only active pipeline as of 2026-05-30. The legacy 7-stage pipeline and
339	OpenRouter inference path were removed. Entrypoint: `pipeline-v2.ts::runIssueAgentV2`.
340	
341	One invocation of `runIssueAgentV2` is one job = one resumable step:
342	
343	```
344	runIssueAgentV2(supabase, userId, issueId)
345	  │
346	  ├─ 1. executeApprovedAction — execute any pending tier-2/3 action,
347	  │      persist result to issue_evidence_items, post system message
348	  │
349	  ├─ 2. Stage 1 (first turn only) — gather telemetry, dedupe, persist
350	  │      lossless set to issue_evidence_items
351	  │
352	  ├─ 3. Stage 2 — one agentic reasoning turn with read-only tool loop
353	  │      → one of: continue | propose_remediation | ask_user |
354	  │                blocked_on_issue | resolved | stuck
355	  │
356	  └─ 4. Stage 3 (on resolved/stuck) — operator message + durable memories
357	```
358	
359	### Stage 1 — Lossless Structurer (`ai/stage1-structurer.ts`)
360	
361	Deterministic, no model call. Runs once per issue on the first turn.
362	
363	**Input:** raw telemetry from `gatherTelemetryContext` (`issue-agent.ts`), which
364	queries these tables in parallel: `alerts`, `nas_logs` (general + high-signal
365	sources), `process_snapshots`, `disk_io_stats`, `scheduled_tasks`, `backup_tasks`,
366	`snapshot_replicas`, `container_io`, `sync_task_snapshots`, `metrics`,
367	`storage_snapshots`, `dsm_errors`.
368	
369	**Processing:** `telemetryToRawItems` maps each table to a `RawEvidenceItem` using
370	the `TELEMETRY_SOURCES` map. For structured rows (process snapshots, container I/O,
371	disk I/O), the full JSON of the row becomes the evidence `body` — no fields are
372	stripped into metadata, ensuring `cpu_pct`, `state`, `read_bps`, `write_bps`, and
373	`queue_depth` are visible to Stage 2.
374	
375	`dedupeEvidence` collapses byte-identical `(source, body)` pairs into groups with
376	`{ dedup_count, first_ts, last_ts }`. Each distinct event is kept verbatim.
377	
378	`classifyEvidence` marks each item `in_scope` (matches `affected_nas` or is
379	severity ≥ error) and `anomalous` (severity ≥ error, or warning with a state-change
380	keyword like "failed", "timeout", "crash").
381	
382	**Output:**
383	1. Full deduped set persisted to `issue_evidence_items` (lossless store,
384	   idempotent — deletes prior rows for the issue first).
385	2. A bounded prioritized evidence slice for the Stage 2 prompt:
386	   - Budget: 12,000 tokens / 48,000 chars.
387	   - 70% of budget: in-scope/anomalous events rendered verbatim.
388	   - 30% of budget: high-volume noise rendered as dedup-with-count summaries.
389	   - Always includes the evidence index (source × hour bucket, sorted by event count).
390	   - If the priority set exceeds budget, a visible truncation notice is appended
391	     (never silent).
392	
393	Stage 2 can retrieve any item from the lossless store at any time via
394	`fetch_evidence`, which reads `issue_evidence_items` — not the raw tables.
395	
396	**Why Stage 1 is deterministic:** the previous issue-agent compressed logs with a
397	model before the reasoner saw them. That made the pipeline lossy: raw log messages
398	were shortened, repeated-but-distinct events were summarized away, and the
399	reasoning stages could miss the evidence that would falsify a bad hypothesis.
400	Stage 1 deliberately preserves every distinct `(source, body)` item and only
401	deduplicates byte-identical repetitions. The prompt slice is bounded, but the DB
402	store is the lossless source of truth.
403	
404	### Stage 2 — Reasoning Core (`ai/stage2-reasoning.ts`)
405	
406	One agentic turn per job invocation. Model: operator-configured via `ai_settings`
407	table (`stage_reasoning_model`); default `claude-sonnet-4-6` / high effort.
408	
409	**Context is rebuilt from the DB on every turn** (resumable invariant): the worker
410	that picks up a job may be a different process after an approval gate or restart.
411	Three parallel loads:
412	
413	1. `getReasoningConfig()` — model + effort from `ai_settings`
414	2. `loadEvidenceSlice(supabase, issueId)` — reads `issue_evidence_items`, rebuilds the
415	   bounded slice without re-running ingestion
416	3. `buildWholeSystemSnapshot(supabase, userId, issue)` — parallel: active alerts
417	   (6h), open issues, NAS reachability probe (3s timeout to primary NAS)
418	4. `loadTranscript(supabase, userId, issueId)` — last 60 `issue_messages` rows
419	   mapped to `{role, content}` pairs
420	
421	**Prompt assembly (stable → dynamic for cache correctness):**
422	
423	| Block | Stability | Content |
424	|---|---|---|
425	| `system` | stable | `SYSTEM_PROMPT` constant — operating rules, tool inventory |
426	| `output_schema` | stable | `OUTPUT_SCHEMA` constant — required JSON fields and decision values |
427	| `taxonomy` | stable | `NAS_TAXONOMY` constant — issue families, iowait interpretation guide, DSM blind spots |
428	| `snapshot` | semi-stable | NAS reachability, active alert counts, open sibling issues |
429	| `issue` | semi-stable | Issue title, severity, status, affected_nas, current hypothesis |
430	| `evidence` | dynamic | Bounded evidence slice from `loadEvidenceSlice` |
431	| `instruction` | dynamic | Per-turn instruction; includes re-chew guard text when triggered |
432	
433	Stable blocks are passed to `context-compiler.ts::block.stable()`, which marks them
434	for prefix caching. Only the last two blocks change per turn.
435	
436	**Tool catalog available to Stage 2 (all tier-1, auto-execute):**
437	
438	- `fetch_evidence` — queries `issue_evidence_items` for this issue; aggregation
439	  (`group_by`) or pagination (`page`, `filter`). Works when NAS is offline.
440	- `run_command` — free-form shell command validated at tier 1 by nas-api. Results
441	  are persisted to `issue_evidence_items` with `source: "tool:run_command"`.
442	- All `write:false` tools from `ALL_TOOL_DEFS` (packages/shared/src/nas-tools.ts)
443	  with the `target` parameter removed from the schema (it is inferred from
444	  `issue.affected_nas[0]`). Tool results are also persisted to
445	  `issue_evidence_items` with `source: "tool:<name>"`.
446	
447	Write tools (`def.write === true`) are excluded entirely from Stage 2's tool
448	catalog. The model proposes write actions via the `propose_remediation` terminal;
449	the operator approves them; `executeApprovedAction` in `pipeline-v2.ts` runs them
450	on the next job invocation.
451	
452	**Tool-use discipline:** Stage 2 should prefer predefined read-only tools when
453	they cover the question because they have tuned command shapes and timeouts. Use
454	`run_command` only for precise read-only probes that are not covered by the catalog.
455	Use `fetch_evidence` aggregation first (`group_by`) before paging raw rows when
456	the evidence store may be large. Do not re-run a tool whose result is already in
457	`issue_evidence_items` unless the live value is expected to have changed and the
458	new sample is diagnostically meaningful.
459	
460	**Re-chew guard:** each turn, Stage 2 hashes the evidence slice text. If the hash
461	matches the previous turn's hash and `decision=continue` is returned again, a
462	`rechew_repeat` counter increments in `issue.metadata`. After ≥2 repeats the
463	per-turn instruction becomes a re-chew guard message, and if the model still returns
464	`continue` after 2 repeats, `toTurnOutcome` forces `decision=ask_user`.
465	
466	**Turn cap:** `TURN_CAP` (from `stage2-turn.ts`) bounds the total number of turns.
467	If exceeded, Stage 2 returns `stuck` without calling the model.
468	
469	**Terminal mapping** (`applyTurnOutcome` in `stage2-turn.ts`):
470	
471	| `decision` | Outcome | Issue status |
472	|---|---|---|
473	| `continue` | enqueue next job | `running` |
474	| `propose_remediation` | persist `issue_actions` row with command/tier/target/summary | `waiting_for_approval` |
475	| `ask_user` | post `issue_messages` with question | `waiting_on_user` |
476	| `blocked_on_issue` | record dependency | `waiting_on_issue` |
477	| `resolved` | close issue; trigger Stage 3 | `resolved` |
478	| `stuck` | close issue; trigger Stage 3 | `stuck` |
479	
480	**Model fit:** Stage 2 needs the strongest reasoning model in the pipeline. The
481	important capabilities are reliable tool calling, long-context coherence,
482	structured JSON output, calibrated uncertainty, and the ability to form and test a
483	ranked hypothesis across several resumable turns. Low-latency or prose quality is
484	less important than converging in a small number of turns without proposing unsafe
485	or vague remediations. Domain familiarity with Linux storage, Btrfs, `/proc`, and
486	Synology DSM helps, but the taxonomy prompt is intended to make a strong general
487	reasoner effective.
488	
489	### Stage 3 — Explainer / Memory (`ai/stage3-explainer.ts`)
490	
491	Single-shot, cheap, low-effort. Model: `stage_explainer_model` from `ai_settings`;
492	default `gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview` / low effort. Runs after Stage 2 reaches
493	`resolved` or `stuck`. Best-effort — a Stage 3 failure never fails the resolution.
494	
495	**Input:** two parallel reads from Supabase:
496	- `issue_evidence_items` where `in_scope=true`, ordered by `ts` desc, limit 30
497	- `issue_actions` for this issue, limit 20 (command, summary, status, result excerpt)
498	
499	**Prompt:** three blocks:
500	- `system` (stable): "write the closing operator update and extract durable knowledge"
501	- `output_schema` (stable): JSON schema for `operator_message` and `memories[]`
502	- `issue_context` (dynamic): JSON blob of issue title, final hypothesis, conversation
503	  summary, evidence highlights, and action results
504	
505	**Output:**
506	1. `operator_message` (2–5 sentences, plain language) posted to `issue_messages` as
507	   role `agent`.
508	2. Up to 5 `agent_memory` entries inserted into `agent_memory` table with
509	   `source_issue_id`. Valid `memory_type` values: `nas_profile`, `issue_pattern`,
510	   `calibration`, `institutional`. Empty memories are skipped. `nas_id: null` means
511	   the lesson is universal across both NASes.
512	
513	Memories are loaded at the start of Stage 2 investigations via
514	`agent-memory-store.ts::loadMemoriesForIssue`, classified by subject so only
515	relevant topics are included (e.g. HyperBackup memories for backup issues).
516	
517	### Stage model selection (live, de-curated)
518	
519	The per-stage model dropdowns in the AI Stages settings panel are populated
520	**live** from every connected provider — a provider counts as connected when its
521	API key env (`ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`, `OPENAI_API_KEY`, `GEMINI_API_KEY`,
522	`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY`, `DASHSCOPE_API_KEY`) is present. `provider-models.ts` calls
523	each connected provider's list-models endpoint (cached in-process ~10 min) and the
524	`/api/ai-models` route serves the union to the UI. `MODEL_CATALOG` in
525	`packages/shared/src/ai-capabilities.ts` is no longer the menu — it is a
526	precise-metadata **override** (hand-verified effort/cache/tool-use per model) and
527	the offline/no-keys fallback.
528	
529	At runtime, `callModel` resolves a model id through a three-step chain
530	(`resolveModelDescriptor` → `resolveLiveDescriptor`):
531	
532	1. **catalog** — exact match in `MODEL_CATALOG` (verified metadata),
533	2. **derived** — for catalog-miss ids, a descriptor inferred from the id (provider
534	   by prefix; cache style per provider; effort/tool-use by conservative id
535	   patterns),
536	3. **live map** — for off-pattern ids (e.g. third-party models hosted on
537	   DashScope), the provider recovered from the live provider→models map.
538	
539	It only throws when no connected provider offers the id. A selected model whose
540	capabilities are *derived* (not catalog-backed) is flagged with an amber "inferred
541	model" warning in the UI, because its effort knob and tool-use support are
542	best-guess until a catalog row is added. Capability gating per stage (Stage 2
543	requires tool use; all stages require structured output) still applies to the live
544	list.
545	
546	**Model fit:** Stage 3 is a communication and memory-distillation task, not a
547	diagnostic task. It benefits from clear writing, good compression, and the ability
548	to extract specific reusable lessons. It does not need live tools, long multi-turn
549	state, or high reasoning effort. A cheaper model is acceptable because Stage 3 is
550	best-effort and must never undo Stage 2's terminal outcome.
551

555	
556	```
557	NAS hardware / DSM APIs
558	        │
559	        │ (each collector at its interval)
560	        ▼
561	agent collectors (Go) ──────────────────────────────┐
562	        │ Queue* methods                             │
563	        ▼                                            │
564	SQLite WAL (/app/data/wal.db)                        │
565	        │ sender.go flush every 30s                  │
566	        │ PostgREST batch insert                     │
567	        ▼                                            │
568	Supabase telemetry tables                            │
569	  metrics, nas_logs, disk_io_stats,                  │
570	  process_snapshots, backup_tasks, ...               │
571	        │                                            │
572	        │ issue-detector.ts (fingerprint)            │
573	        ▼                                            │
574	issues table (one row per grouped problem)           │
575	        │                                            │
576	        │ pipeline-v2.ts job                         │
577	        ▼                                            │
578	Stage 1: gatherTelemetryContext ─────────────────────┘
579	  → telemetryToRawItems
580	  → dedupeEvidence + classifyEvidence
581	  → persistEvidenceItems (issue_evidence_items)
582	  → buildEvidenceSlice
583	        │
584	        ▼
585	Stage 2: runStage2Turn (agentic loop)
586	  context: evidence slice + transcript + snapshot
587	  tools: fetch_evidence, run_command, 100+ NAS tools
588	  each tool result → issue_evidence_items
589	  terminal → issue_actions (remediation) or status change
590	        │
591	        │ (on resolved/stuck)
592	        ▼
593	Stage 3: runStage3Explainer (single-shot)
594	  → operator_message → issue_messages
595	  → agent_memory entries
596	```

600	## Database
601	
602	Supabase project `qnjimovrsaacneqkggsn`. 53 tables total (migrations 00001–00041).
603	
604	**Partitioned tables** (pg_partman, monthly, auto-retention): `metrics`,
605	`nas_logs`, `storage_snapshots`, `container_status`, `drive_activities`.
606	
607	**Key table groups:**
608	
609	| Group | Tables |
610	|---|---|
611	| Telemetry | `metrics`, `nas_logs`, `disk_io_stats`, `process_snapshots`, `net_connections`, `container_io`, `backup_tasks`, `scheduled_tasks`, `snapshot_replicas`, `service_health`, `dsm_errors`, `package_status`, `security_events`, `drive_activities`, `sync_task_snapshots`, `drive_team_folders` |
612	| Issue pipeline | `issues`, `issue_messages`, `issue_evidence`, `issue_evidence_items`, `issue_actions`, `issue_jobs`, `issue_stage_runs`, `issue_state_transitions`, `agent_memory` |
613	| AI config | `ai_settings`, `ai_model_calls` |
614	| Copilot / resolution | `copilot_sessions`, `copilot_messages`, `copilot_actions`, `sync_remediations` |
615	| Custom collection | `custom_metric_schedules`, `custom_metric_data` |
616	
617	**`issue_evidence` vs `issue_evidence_items` — different tables, different purposes:**
618	
619	- `issue_evidence` (migration 00022): curated human-readable notes (title/detail)
620	  written by the copilot, the resolution API, and `seedIssueFromOrigin`. Not used
621	  by the 3-stage pipeline.
622	- `issue_evidence_items` (migration 00038): the lossless telemetry store for the
623	  3-stage pipeline. Written by Stage 1 and by every Stage 2 tool call. Read by
624	  Stage 2 `fetch_evidence` and Stage 3. Do not query the wrong one.
625	
626	**`package_status`** is the only merge-duplicates upsert (current state, one row
627	per NAS+package). All other telemetry tables are append-only inserts.
628	
629	**AI settings** (`ai_settings` table) must be read via `createAdminClient()`
630	(service role). The issue agent runs as a background worker with no user session;
631	the session client returns empty under RLS, silently falling back to hardcoded
632	defaults.

635	
636	## Key design constraints and intentional decisions
637	
638	### No source whitelists on `nas_logs` / `alerts`
639	
640	`CHECK` constraints that enumerated allowed source values were dropped in migration
641	00035. They had to be hand-expanded every time a collector added a source, and one
642	bad row failing the whole PostgREST batch silently froze ingestion for ~19h/23d.
643	The agent governs what it writes; the sender isolates bad rows.
644	
645	### NAS MCP is fully stateless (per-request McpServer)
646	
647	Every HTTP request builds a new `McpServer`, handles the request, discards it.
648	`sessionIdGenerator: undefined`, `enableJsonResponse: true`. Stateful mode brings
649	back session-resume 404s after Coolify restarts, and the claude.ai proxy's
650	4-minute hang was traced to a stateful `GET /mcp` without a session ID.
651	
652	### NAS MCP exposes 5 tools but has a 119-definition registry
653	
654	Pre-loading 119 schemas puts ~50k tokens into every session and degrades it after
655	~10–15 calls. Lazy-load via `tool_search` + `invoke_tool` keeps the always-on
656	surface ~3k tokens. `notifications/tools/list_changed` is not used because Claude
657	clients cache the initial `tools/list` and do not re-fetch on the notification.
658	
659	### HMAC approval tokens are never persisted
660	
661	Stage 2 persists the action intent (command, tier, target, summary) in
662	`issue_actions` but never the HMAC token. The token is minted fresh in
663	`pipeline-v2.ts::executeApprovedAction` at execution time. Persisting tokens would
664	cause 403s after the 15-minute expiry; the operator's approval window is often hours.
665	
666	### Stage 2 tool results go into `issue_evidence_items`
667	
668	Every `run_command` and predefined NAS tool call in Stage 2 inserts a row into
669	`issue_evidence_items` with `source: "tool:<name>"`. This means later turns can
670	page the result via `fetch_evidence` without re-running the command — and the
671	Stage 3 explainer sees the full investigative trail without needing a separate
672	transcript of tool outputs.
673	
674	### Prompt order: stable → dynamic
675	
676	`context-compiler.ts` enforces that system/output-schema/taxonomy blocks come
677	before snapshot/issue/evidence/instruction blocks. This keeps the cacheable prefix
678	maximally long (stable blocks are identical across all issues and all turns), so
679	only the evidence slice and per-turn instruction incur full token costs.
680	
681	### Provider cache observability matters
682	
683	The issue agent calls providers directly so provider-specific cache controls and
684	usage fields remain visible. Do not route the cached inference path through an
685	aggregator that flattens request/response shapes. A thin wrapper for timeouts,
686	retry classification, and normalized accounting is fine, but raw provider usage
687	must still be persisted or inspectable.
688	
689	Provider-specific details that have caused bugs in similar systems:
690	- Anthropic explicit cache reads/writes are reported as
691	  `cache_read_input_tokens` and `cache_creation_input_tokens`; extended thinking
692	  requires the provider's required temperature shape.
693	- OpenAI caches stable prefixes automatically; cache reads surface under
694	  `prompt_tokens_details.cached_tokens`.
695	- DeepSeek's cache fields are `prompt_cache_hit_tokens` and
696	  `prompt_cache_miss_tokens`, not the OpenAI names.
697	- Qwen/DashScope multi-turn cache behavior depends on preserving the provider's
698	  response/session id between turns.
699	- Gemini explicit cached content, if used, must have a tracked lifecycle so paid
700	  caches are cleaned up even when workers restart.
701	
702	Cache is an optimization only. Every Stage 2 turn must rebuild its context from
703	the database transcript, evidence store, issue state, and settings. A cache miss
704	may cost more tokens, but must not change behavior.
705	
706	### `connection: close` on all NAS API HTTP calls
707	
708	Undici's keep-alive pool exhausts after ~10–15 calls when timed-out requests do
709	not return their socket. NAS API is reached over Tailscale (sub-ms RTT), making
710	re-handshake cost negligible.
711	
712	### Executor kills the process group
713	
714	`executor.go` uses `cmd.SysProcAttr{Setpgid: true}` and
715	`syscall.Kill(-pid, SIGKILL)` on timeout. `exec.CommandContext` kills only the
716	direct bash child; without process-group kill, `grep ... | head` orphans the
717	`grep` subprocess. Combined with the hard-block on recursive grep against Synology
718	internal stores, this prevents the runaway that ran for 4d11h on production.
719	
720	### `NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_*` baked at build time
721	
722	These variables are passed as Docker build args in `web-image.yml` and embedded
723	into the Next.js client bundle at `docker build` time. Setting them in Coolify
724	after the image is built has no effect. A new push to `main` is required to change
725	them.
726	
727	---
728	
729	## Known constraints and blind spots
730	
731	- `container_status` CPU/mem always reads 0 — use `container_io` instead
732	- `scheduled_tasks` returns DSM error 103 on edgesynology1 (unsupported API version)
733	- Some snapshot-replication APIs are unsupported on certain DSM versions
734	- Log-derived fields are regex-parsed — categorizations imperfect, raw text faithful
735	- `analyzeRecentLogs` in `log-analyzer.ts` has no callers; tables preserved for a
736	  future AI clustering layer
737	- `second_opinion_model` and `cluster_model` exist in `ai-settings.ts` but are not
738	  wired to any pipeline stage
739	- `drive_team_folders` is written by the agent but never queried by the web app
740	- `drive_team_folders_partitioned` has no child partitions and receives no writes;
741	  it is forward infrastructure — do not drop it
742	- `issue_resolutions` / `resolution_steps` / `resolution_log` / `resolution_messages`
743	  are superseded by the `issues` pipeline but not yet dropped
744

747	## Relay (`apps/relay`)
748	
749	A narrow named-action HTTP proxy for an external (Lovable) frontend. No CI
750	workflow exists — there is no `.github/workflows/relay-*.yml`. The relay image is
751	built and deployed manually on the VPS. Treat this as an exceptional deploy path,
752	not the routine one. See `apps/relay/OPERATIONS.md`.

3	## Prerequisites
4	
5	- **Go 1.23+** with CGO enabled (agent requires `go-sqlite3`; nas-api does not but uses the same toolchain)
6	- **Node.js 22+** and **pnpm 9** (web app and nas-mcp)
7	- **Docker** (to run Supabase locally or build images)
8	- `gcc` and `musl-dev` (or equivalent libc headers) for CGO builds on Linux/Alpine
9	- Tailscale connected (for live NAS API calls from dev; not needed if using stored evidence only)
10	- Supabase CLI (`npm i -g supabase`) for local migration management
11	
12	## Local setup
13	
14	```sh
15	# From repo root
16	pnpm install          # install all workspace deps
17	pnpm build            # build all packages in dependency order (turbo)
18	                      # pipeline: shared:build → web:build, nas-mcp:build
19	```
20	
21	For the web app, copy the example env file and fill in values:
22	
23	```sh
24	cp apps/web/.env.example apps/web/.env.local

252	## Workflow: adding a new NAS tool
253	
254	This is the most common extension task. A "NAS tool" is a predefined diagnostic or
255	remediation command the MCP server and/or the Stage 2 issue agent can invoke.
256	
257	### Step 1 — Define the tool in `packages/shared/src/nas-tools.ts`
258	
259	Add an entry to `ALL_TOOL_DEFS`:
260	
261	```typescript
262	{
263	  name: "my_new_tool",
264	  description: "What it does — be specific, Stage 2 picks tools by description.",
265	  write: false,          // true if it modifies NAS state (tier 2/3)
266	  params: {
267	    target,              // import the shared target param
268	    // add any other zod params your command needs
269	    filter: z.string().optional().describe("optional search term"),
270	  },
271	  buildCommand: (input) => [
272	    "echo '=== MY TOOL ==='",
273	    `grep -r ${quote(String(input.filter ?? ""))} /var/log/my-log.log | tail -n 50`,
274	  ].join("\n"),
275	},
276	```
277	
278	Add it to `TOOL_GROUPS` in the same file so `tool_search` can find it by group:
279	
280	```typescript
281	const TOOL_GROUPS: Record<string, string> = {
282	  // ... existing entries ...
283	  my_new_tool: "logs",   // pick the closest group
284	};
285	```
286	
287	If you skip `TOOL_GROUPS`, the tool falls into `"misc"` and startup logs a warning.
288	It still works, but won't surface in group-based searches.
289	
290	Path rule for NAS tools: commands run inside the `synology-monitor-nas-api`
291	container. Do not assume the telemetry agent's mount layout. Package state is
292	mounted at `/host/packages`, DSM binaries at `/host/usr/syno`, DSM logs at
293	`/host/log`, and the full Btrfs volume at `/btrfs/volumeN`. Individual shared
294	folders are mounted under `/volume1/<share>`, but that is not enough for snapshot
295	enumeration. If DSM 7 hides scheduler or Snapshot Replication details behind
296	package state or WebAPI, prefer narrow read-only path discovery plus DSM WebAPI
297	`list`/`query` methods over broad filesystem scans.
298	
299	### Step 2 — Enable the tool in `apps/nas-mcp/tools-config.json`
300	
301	Add the tool name to `enabled_read_tools` (read-only) or `enabled_write_tools`
302	(state-modifying):
303	
304	```json
305	{
306	  "enabled_read_tools": [
307	    "...",
308	    "my_new_tool"
309	  ]
310	}
311	```
312	
313	A tool present in `ALL_TOOL_DEFS` but absent from `tools-config.json` is invisible
314	to `tool_search` and rejected by `invoke_tool` with a "disabled" message. This lets
315	you ship a tool dark and enable it without a code deploy.
316	
317	### Step 3 — For write tools: update the NAS API validator
318	
319	If `write: true`, the validator at `apps/nas-api/internal/validator/validator.go`
320	must be able to classify the generated command at the right tier.
321	
322	- **Tier 2** (service ops — reversible, no `/volume*` touch): the command probably
323	  already matches a `writePatterns` regex. Verify with a test.
324	- **Tier 3** (file ops — touches `/volume*`): ensure a `filePatterns` entry matches.
325	- **Allowlisted Docker compose commands**: add to `allowedServiceCommands` in
326	  `validator.go` if it's a `docker compose ...` variant.
327	
328	Always add a test in `apps/nas-api/internal/validator/validator_test.go`:
329	
330	```go
331	{name: "my_new_tool tier2", command: "synopkg restart MyPackage", wantTier: 2},
332	```
333	
334	Regex rule: `apps/nas-api` is Go, so `regexp.MustCompile` patterns must be valid
335	Go/RE2 regular expressions. Do not use negative lookahead like `(?!...)` to make
336	exceptions. Express the positive match instead, or write a small Go helper and test
337	both the matching and exception cases.
338	
339	Run:
340	```sh
341	cd apps/nas-api && go test ./internal/validator/...
342	```
343	
344	If the local machine lacks Go, use the same containerized test command as CI/debug
345	sessions:
346	
347	```sh
348	docker run --rm -v "$PWD/../..:/src" -w /src/apps/nas-api golang:1.23-alpine \
349	  go test ./internal/validator/...
350	```
351	
352	### Step 4 — Stage 2 picks it up automatically
353	
354	Stage 2's `buildStage2Tools()` in `stage2-reasoning.ts` loads all
355	`ALL_TOOL_DEFS.filter(def => !def.write)` automatically. No registration needed.
356	Write tools never appear in Stage 2 (the agent proposes remediations; it does not
357	auto-execute tier-2/3 actions).
358	
359	### Step 5 — Build and verify
360	
361	```sh
362	pnpm build            # from repo root — compiles shared, web, nas-mcp
363	pnpm type-check
364	```
365	
366	Push to `main`. `nas-mcp-image.yml` triggers on changes to `apps/nas-mcp/**` and
367	`packages/shared/**` and redeploys the MCP server. `web-image.yml` also watches
368	`packages/shared/**`, so shared tool changes may build/redeploy the web image too.
369	
370	## Workflow: adding a new metric or telemetry type
371	
372	This is the pipeline for getting a new data source into the issue agent's evidence
373	store and prompt.
374	
375	### Step 1 — Agent collector
376	
377	Create `apps/agent/internal/collector/yourname.go`. The collector must:
378	
379	1. Implement a `Run(stop <-chan struct{})` method.
380	2. Use the WaitGroup pattern when wired in `main.go`:
381	
382	   ```go
383	   wg.Add(1)
384	   go func() {
385	       defer wg.Done()
386	       yourCollector.Run(stop)
387	   }()
388	   ```
389	
390	   Omitting `wg.Add(1)` means graceful shutdown returns before the collector
391	   finishes, silently dropping in-flight WAL writes. This exact bug existed in the
392	   ShareSync collector (see AGENTS.md §10).
393	
394	3. Call `sender.Queue*` to buffer rows. Add a payload type to `sender/types.go`
395	   and a `Queue*` method to `sender/sender.go` if this is a new table.
396	
397	4. To persist a cursor between restarts, use `sender.SaveCheckpoint` /
398	   `sender.LoadCheckpoint` (writes to the `checkpoints` SQLite table in the WAL).
399	
400	5. Access host kernel data at `/host/proc/...` and `/host/sys/...` — the NAS
401	   compose mounts `/proc:/host/proc:ro` and `/sys:/host/sys:ro`. See `diskstats.go`
402	   and `sysextras.go` for examples.
403	
404	**Do not add a sender payload field without a matching Supabase column.** The
405	PostgREST batch will reject the row and trigger poison-row isolation (5 retries,
406	then drop).
407	
408	### Step 2 — Database migration
409	
410	Add a migration for the new table or column:
411	
412	```sh
413	# Next number after 00041:
414	supabase/migrations/00042_add_my_new_table.sql
415	```
416	
417	For a new time-series table, follow the pattern in existing migrations for
418	pg_partman monthly partitioning. Applied migrations are immutable — never edit
419	them.
420	
421	### Step 3 — Supabase sender
422	
423	If the new data belongs in an existing table (e.g. a new `type` in `metrics`), no
424	schema change is needed. If it is a new table:
425	
426	- Add to `upsertTables` in `sender/sender.go` **only** if the table uses
427	  merge-on-conflict semantics (like `package_status`). All other tables are
428	  append-only inserts.
429	- Add the table name to the Go sender payload types in `sender/types.go`.
430	
431	### Step 4 — `gatherTelemetryContext` in `issue-agent.ts`
432	
433	Add a Supabase query for the new table in `apps/web/src/lib/server/issue-agent.ts`
434	inside the `Promise.all([...])` block:
435	
436	```typescript
437	supabase
438	  .from("my_new_table")
439	  .select("nas_id, captured_at, my_field, another_field")
440	  .gte("captured_at", since6h)
441	  .order("captured_at", { ascending: false })
442	  .limit(20),
443	```
444	
445	Collect the result:
446	
447	```typescript
448	const myNewData = collectResult("my_new_data", myNewResult, telemetry_errors);
449	```
450	
451	Return it from the function:
452	
453	```typescript
454	return {
455	  // ... existing fields ...
456	  my_new_data: myNewData,
457	};
458	```
459	
460	### Step 5 — Stage 1 `TELEMETRY_SOURCES` in `stage1-structurer.ts`
461	
462	Add a `SourceMap` entry to the `TELEMETRY_SOURCES` array in
463	`apps/web/src/lib/server/ai/stage1-structurer.ts`:
464	
465	```typescript
466	{
467	  key: "my_new_data",         // must match the key returned by gatherTelemetryContext
468	  source: "my_source_label",  // appears in evidence bodies as "source/severity: body"
469	  tsFields: ["captured_at"],  // fields tried in order for the timestamp
470	  // bodyFields: ["field1", "field2"], // optional: joined with " — "; omit to use full JSON
471	},
472	```
473	
474	**Omit `bodyFields` when diagnostic numbers must be visible.** If you use
475	`bodyFields`, only those fields form the evidence body — metric values land in
476	`metadata` which `fetch_evidence` never returns, making the data invisible to Stage
477	2. See the comment on `top_processes` in the source file.
478	
479	Stage 1 runs automatically before every Stage 2 turn. Once added here, the new data
480	surfaces in `issue_evidence_items` and the bounded evidence slice that Stage 2
481	receives in its prompt.
482	
483	### Step 6 — Stage 2 taxonomy (optional)
484	
485	If the new data requires interpretation rules (e.g. thresholds, DSM blind spots,
486	known false-positive patterns), add them to the `NAS_TAXONOMY` constant in
487	`apps/web/src/lib/server/ai/stage2-reasoning.ts`. Keep additions concise — the
488	taxonomy is in the stable cacheable prefix, so additions persist across all turns.
489	
490	### Step 7 — Verify the pipeline
491	
492	```sh
493	pnpm type-check       # catch type mismatches across issue-agent / stage1 / stage2
494	```
495	
496	Check that a new issue run populates `issue_evidence_items` with the expected
497	`source` label. The Supabase dashboard or `fetch_evidence` (from Stage 2) can
498	confirm rows are landing.
499	
500	## Debugging
501	
502	### Agent not sending data
503	
504	1. Check `/app/data/wal.db` size — if growing, data is queuing but not flushing.
505	2. Check agent container logs: `docker logs synology-monitor-agent`.
506	3. Check `nas_logs` and `metrics` freshness in Supabase. If metrics are fresh but
507	   logs are stale, one source is hitting a bad row — check the sender log for
508	   `[sender] isolating bad row` lines.
509	4. An empty table is a bug, not a healthy state. A collector may be hitting an
510	   unsupported DSM API — check `nas_logs` for `API unavailable` or error 103 entries.
511	
512	### NAS API not responding
513	
514	1. Check `docker logs synology-monitor-nas-api` on the NAS.
515	2. Verify Tailscale is connected: the web app reaches NAS API over Tailscale IPs
516	   (`100.107.131.35:7734` / `100.107.131.36:7734`).
517	3. Health check: `GET http://<nas-tailscale-ip>:7734/health` returns `{"status":"ok"}`.
518	   This endpoint requires no auth.
519	4. A silent 403 on every request usually means the `NAS_API_SECRET` in Coolify does
520	   not match `NAS_API_SECRET` in the NAS `.env` — credential parity mismatch.
521	5. `ECONNREFUSED` means the NAS is reachable but nothing is listening on `:7734`.
522	   Check `docker ps -a` and `docker logs synology-monitor-nas-api --tail 100`. If
523	   the logs show a Go panic from `regexp.MustCompile`, fix the validator regex,
524	   push, wait for `nas-api-image.yml`, then pull/recreate `nas-api` on both NASes.
525	
526	### Stage 2 / issue-agent not running
527	
528	1. Check `issue_jobs` table — are jobs being enqueued with `status='pending'`?
529	2. Check `ISSUE_WORKER_MODE` env var:
530	   - `inline` — jobs drain on the next API request to the dashboard
531	   - `background` — a separate worker loop drains via `ISSUE_WORKER_TOKEN`
532	3. Check `ai_model_calls` table for model errors (rate limits, invalid key, context
533	   window exceeded).
534	4. Check `issue_stage_runs` for per-stage failure messages.
535	
536	### Stage 1 evidence missing
537	
538	If Stage 2 says evidence is empty but data is in the DB:
539	
540	1. Check `issue_evidence_items` for the issue ID — Stage 1 replaces rows on each
541	   run (idempotent delete + insert).
542	2. Check the `source` label in `issue_evidence_items` against `TELEMETRY_SOURCES`
543	   in `stage1-structurer.ts` — a mismatch in the `key` field silently skips the
544	   source.
545	3. Check `gatherTelemetryContext` return value — the key must match exactly.
546	
547	### NAS API validator rejecting a command
548	
549	Run the validator tests with the new command to diagnose the tier:
550	
551	```sh
552	cd apps/nas-api && go test ./internal/validator/... -v
553	```
554	
555	`ClassifyTier` returns `-1` for hard-blocked, `1` for read-only, `2` for service
556	ops, `3` for file ops. Add a targeted test case rather than guessing at regex
557	matches.
558	
559	If the validator change introduces a new `regexp.MustCompile`, first confirm the
560	pattern is valid Go/RE2 syntax. A bad pattern will not merely reject a command; it
561	will panic at process start and take the whole NAS API offline.
562	
563	### Type errors
564	
565	```sh
566	pnpm type-check
567	```
568	
569	The most common causes:
570	
571	- A new DB column added without a matching TypeScript type annotation.
572	- A `fetch()` call without proper response typing.
573	- A new `gatherTelemetryContext` return field not reflected in `TELEMETRY_SOURCES`.
574	- The `shared` package not rebuilt after changing `nas-tools.ts` — run `pnpm build`
575	  from the repo root to regenerate `packages/shared/dist/`.
576	
577	### Build: `guard:ai` CI check failing
578	
579	The web app has an AI cache guard (`pnpm --filter @synology-monitor/web run guard:ai`)
580	that enforces stable→dynamic prompt block ordering and normalized provider usage
581	fields. If the CI build fails at this step, run the guard locally:
582	
583	```sh
584	cd apps/web && pnpm run guard:ai
585	```
586	
587	The error message identifies which file and which invariant failed.

589	## WAL mechanism
590	
591	If the agent cannot reach Supabase (network outage, maintenance), all telemetry
592	queues in `/app/data/wal.db` on the NAS disk. The WAL is capped at
593	`MAX_WAL_SIZE_MB` (default 100 MB) — oldest entries are dropped at the cap. Once
594	connectivity resumes, the agent flushes the backlog automatically.
595	
596	The `checkpoints` table in the same SQLite file stores durable collector cursors
597	(log file byte offsets, ShareSync watermarks, etc.) so collectors resume from the
598	right position after restarts.
599	
600	**Poison-row isolation:** on a PostgREST `4xx`, the sender re-sends each row in
601	the failed batch individually. Good rows land; only the bad row accumulates retries
602	(max 5, then dropped). This was added after a single bad row silently froze log and
603	alert ingestion for 19h and 23 days respectively (see AGENTS.md §13).
604	
605	## Custom metric schedules
606	
607	To schedule a recurring shell command without a code change or deploy:
608	
609	```sql
610	INSERT INTO custom_metric_schedules
611	  (name, description, nas_id, collection_command, interval_minutes, is_active, next_run_at)
612	VALUES
613	  ('my_check', 'What it does', 'edgesynology1', 'cat /proc/loadavg', 60, true, now());
614	```
615	
616	The agent polls every 60s, claims due rows with an optimistic lock, and stores
617	output in `custom_metric_data`. Stage 2 can read recent output via `fetch_evidence`
618	(source: `custom_metric_data` rows surface after the next Stage 1 run for the issue).
619	
620	Available paths in the agent container:
621	
622	| Host path | Container path |
623	|---|---|
624	| `/proc` | `/host/proc` |
625	| `/sys` | `/host/sys` |
626	| `/var/log` | `/host/log` |
627	| `/volume1/@synologydrive` | `/host/shares/@synologydrive` |
628	| `/volume1/@SynologyDriveShareSync` | `/host/shares/@SynologyDriveShareSync` |
629	| (other shares) | `/host/shares/<name>` |
630

3	## Overview
4	
5	- **Production:** Runtime env lives in **Coolify** (VPS apps) and each NAS `.env`
6	  file (agent + nas-api). Do not commit real values or edit production env from the
7	  repo side.
8	- **Development:** Copy the `*.env.example` files and fill in your values locally.
9	- **AI model settings:** Stored in the `ai_settings` Supabase table; editable via
10	  the Settings UI. See [AI model settings](#ai-model-settings) below.

13	
14	## Agent (`deploy/synology/nas-1.env.example`)
15	
16	### Required
17	
18	| Variable | Purpose | Default |
19	|---|---|---|
20	| `NAS_ID` | UUID. Must match `nas_units.id` in Supabase. Validated on startup. | — |
21	| `NAS_NAME` | Display name in the UI | `Synology NAS 1` |
22	| `DSM_URL` | DSM web interface URL | `https://localhost:5001` |

157	## AI model settings
158	
159	Model assignments are stored in the `ai_settings` Supabase table and read at
160	runtime. The Settings UI exposes them. For the **3 pipeline stages**, an empty
161	value falls back directly to the hardcoded `STAGE_DESCRIPTORS` default in
162	`packages/shared/src/ai-capabilities.ts` (no env-var layer); these defaults match
163	the migration 00036 seed. The **copilot** models (`diagnosis_model`,
164	`remediation_model`, `cluster_model`) fall back through env vars then a hardcoded
165	default.
166	
167	**Non-obvious:** `ai_settings` must be read via `createAdminClient()` (service
168	role), not the session client. The issue agent runs as a background worker with no
169	user session; the session client returns `{}` under RLS, silently falling back to
170	hardcoded defaults.
171	
172	| Key | Stage / feature | Fallback |
173	|---|---|---|
174	| `stage_structurer_model` / `stage_structurer_effort` | Stage 1 | `gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview` / `minimal` |
175	| `stage_reasoning_model` / `stage_reasoning_effort` | Stage 2 | `claude-sonnet-4-6` / `high` |
176	| `stage_explainer_model` / `stage_explainer_effort` | Stage 3 | `gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview` / `low` |
177	| `diagnosis_model` | Copilot (MiniMax) | `MINIMAX_MODEL` env → `minimax/minimax-m2.7` |
178	| `remediation_model` | Copilot (OpenAI) | `OPENAI_CHAT_MODEL` env → `openai/gpt-5.4` |
179	| `second_opinion_model` | Planned cross-check feature (not yet wired) | `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4` |
180	| `cluster_model` | Log clustering (writer has no callers) | `diagnosis_model` → `minimax/minimax-m2.7` |
181	
182	### Model capability and effort controls
183	
184	The Settings UI stores a model and an effort/reasoning value per active stage, but
185	"effort" is provider-specific. The provider client should map the abstract setting
186	only when the selected model supports it and omit unsupported parameters rather
187	than sending a no-op or invalid field.
188	
189	| Provider | Effort shape | Notes |
190	|---|---|---|
191	| Anthropic | extended thinking budget | Best fit for Stage 2 when tool use and reasoning are needed; cache usage has Anthropic-specific read/write fields. |
192	| OpenAI | `reasoning_effort` on reasoning models | Prefix caching is automatic; non-reasoning models may not accept the effort parameter. |
193	| Gemini | thinking config where supported | Explicit cached content needs lifecycle tracking if enabled. |
194	| DeepSeek | usually model choice, not a generic knob | Cache usage fields differ from OpenAI-compatible defaults. |
195	| Qwen/DashScope | model-specific; often OpenAI-style | Multi-turn cache behavior may require preserving provider response/session ids. |
196	
197	Stage 2 should be limited to models with reliable tool use, structured JSON output,
198	long-context coherence, and strong multi-step reasoning. Stage 3 can use a cheaper
199	model optimized for concise writing and memory extraction. If a model lacks an
200	effort knob, keep the selected model and treat effort as disabled for that provider.
201	
202	**Model dropdowns are live, not curated.** As of 2026-06-02 the AI-stage model
203	dropdowns are populated from every connected provider's list-models endpoint
204	(`apps/web/src/lib/server/ai/provider-models.ts`, served by `/api/ai-models`,
205	cached ~10 min). So any model a connected provider exposes — including ones newer
206	than this repo — is selectable. A provider is "connected" when its key env is set.
207	
208	`packages/shared/src/ai-capabilities.ts` remains the source of truth for the
209	provider capability matrix, stage requirements, fallback models, and stage spec
210	text — but its `MODEL_CATALOG` is now a precise-metadata **override and offline
211	fallback**, not the menu:
212	
213	- A model id in `MODEL_CATALOG` uses its hand-verified metadata (effort control,
214	  effort levels, cache style, tool use).
215	- A catalog-miss id gets a **derived** descriptor: provider from the id prefix
216	  (`inferProvider`), cache style from the provider, and effort/tool-use from
217	  conservative id-pattern heuristics. Derivation is intentionally cautious about
218	  enabling an effort knob, because sending an unsupported reasoning param 400s the
219	  call while omitting one merely forgoes thinking.
220	- The runtime resolves ids `catalog → derived → live-map` (`resolveModelDescriptor`
221	  then `resolveLiveDescriptor`); it only fails when no connected provider offers
222	  the id.
223	
224	The Settings UI still gates the live list per stage (Stage 2 needs tool use; all
225	stages need structured output), shows an amber **"inferred model"** warning when a
226	selected model's capabilities are derived rather than catalog-backed, and builds
227	the copy-spec clipboard payload for asking another model which provider-native
228	model best fits a stage. **To tune a specific model precisely** (its effort knob or
229	tool-use), add a row to `MODEL_CATALOG` — the catalog always overrides the
230	heuristic. The `OPENROUTER_API_KEY`-backed `/api/models` route is a *separate*
231	dropdown for the copilot, not the 3-stage pipeline.

236

255	## Custom metric schedules
256	
257	Insert rows into `custom_metric_schedules` to schedule recurring shell commands
258	on the agent (no code change or deploy required):
259	
260	| Column | Type | Notes |
261	|---|---|---|
262	| `name` | text | Descriptive name |
263	| `description` | text | What it collects and why |
264	| `nas_id` | text | `edgesynology1` or `edgesynology2` |
265	| `collection_command` | text | Shell command run via `sh -c` in the agent container |
266	| `interval_minutes` | int | How often to run (minimum 1) |
267	| `is_active` | bool | Set false to pause without deleting |
268	| `next_run_at` | timestamptz | When to next run (default: `now()`) |
269	
270	The agent polls every 60s and claims due rows with an optimistic lock.
271	Output is stored in `custom_metric_data.raw_output`.
272	
273	Available paths in the agent container:
274	- `/host/proc/*` — host kernel virtual files
275	- `/host/sys/*` — host sysfs
276	- `/host/log/*` — host `/var/log`
277	- `/host/shares/@synologydrive/*` — Drive appdata
278	- Standard shell utilities: `cat`, `grep`, `awk`, `head`, `tail`, `find`
